target.title: fix(tui): show 0 not ? for fresh-session context tokens in footer
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: On a fresh session the TUI footer renders the context line as `?/1.0m` instead of `0/1.0m`, the same wrong-on-fresh display that #93798 fixed for `/status`.
- Solution: Honor the gateway's `totalTokensFresh` signal in the TUI session-info merge — when the total is absent but flagged fresh, render `0` instead of an unknown `?`.
- What changed: `SessionInfo` gains `totalTokensFresh`; `tui-backend` forwards it from the gateway session payload; the session-info merge maps fresh-but-absent total to `0` and only stale/unknown totals to `null`.
- What did NOT change: stale/unknown sessions still show `?`; no change to how totals are computed, persisted, or to any non-TUI surface.
- Why it matters / User impact: a brand-new session's context usage reads as a correct `0/…` instead of a misleading `?/…`.

## Origin / follow-up

- **Follows / completes:** #93798, which fixed this for `/status`, for the original report #93771. The TUI footer is the sibling surface that #93798 did not cover.
- **Gap this fills:** #93798 added `totalTokensFresh` and fixed the `/status` auto-reply path, but the TUI footer (`src/tui/tui.ts` via `tui-session-actions`) ignored `totalTokensFresh` and still rendered `?` for fresh sessions.
- **Consistency:** Same fresh-vs-stale distinction as #93798 — `totalTokensFresh === true` means known `0`; absent fresh signal means unknown `?`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Fresh-session TUI footer now shows `0/1.0m` instead of `?/1.0m`; stale/unknown sessions still show `?`.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux worktree at current PR head `43657b52c803047d84269256765c0e21ef1bf145`, fetched `origin/main` `8f48f5e5ba67caca2fb13640768484c898fdc123`, Node `v22.22.0`. The real `node scripts/run-node.mjs tui` CLI was launched in a pseudo-terminal with `script`, connected to a local WebSocket gateway protocol stub that supplied session-history payloads.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/followup-93798"
git fetch fork fix/followup-93798
git checkout --detach 43657b52c803047d84269256765c0e21ef1bf145
```

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts -t "totalTokensFresh"
```

```bash
# Fresh session proof
OPENCLAW_TUI_PROOF_SCENARIO=fresh \
  node "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/tui-proof-gateway.mjs" "$PORT_FILE" "$LOG" &

timeout 90s script -q -f -c \
  "OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR='$STATE_DIR' OPENCLAW_HOME='$HOME_DIR' OPENCLAW_THEME=dark \
   TERM=xterm-256color COLUMNS=140 LINES=36 \
   node scripts/run-node.mjs tui --url 'ws://127.0.0.1:$PORT' --token proof-token \
   --session 'proof-fresh' --history-limit 5" \
  "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/fresh-current-head-tui-script.raw"
```

```bash
# Stale/unknown session proof
OPENCLAW_TUI_PROOF_SCENARIO=stale \
  node "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/tui-proof-gateway.mjs" "$PORT_FILE" "$LOG" &

timeout 90s script -q -f -c \
  "OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR='$STATE_DIR' OPENCLAW_HOME='$HOME_DIR' OPENCLAW_THEME=dark \
   TERM=xterm-256color COLUMNS=140 LINES=36 \
   node scripts/run-node.mjs tui --url 'ws://127.0.0.1:$PORT' --token proof-token \
   --session 'proof-stale' --history-limit 5" \
  "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/stale-current-head-tui-script.raw"
```

```bash
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
BASE="$(git merge-base HEAD origin/main)"
git merge-tree "$BASE" HEAD origin/main > "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/merge-tree-origin-main-current-head-20260618.txt"
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Fresh-session protocol payload omitted `totalTokens`, included `totalTokensFresh: true`, and supplied `contextTokens: 1_000_000`; the real TUI footer rendered `0`.

```text
openclaw tui - ws://127.0.0.1:36591 - agent main (Main) - session proof-fresh
agent main (Main) | session proof-fresh | proof-provider/proof-model | tokens 0/1.0m (0%)
```

Probe/stale payload omitted both `totalTokens` and `totalTokensFresh`; the same TUI launch path preserved the unknown display.

```text
openclaw tui - ws://127.0.0.1:33843 - agent main (Main) - session proof-stale
agent main (Main) | session proof-stale | proof-provider/proof-model | tokens ?/1.0m
```

Focused regression output:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 33 skipped (34)
```

Gateway request log confirms the TUI connected through the public gateway request envelope and loaded history through `chat.history`:

```text
fresh: connect -> sessions.subscribe -> agents.list -> chat.history -> commands.list
stale: connect -> sessions.subscribe -> agents.list -> chat.history -> commands.list
```

Saved captures:

```text
/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/fresh-current-head-tui-script.raw
/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/fresh-current-head-tui-script.text
/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/fresh-current-head-gateway.log.jsonl
/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/stale-current-head-tui-script.raw
/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/stale-current-head-tui-script.text
/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94337-evidence/stale-current-head-gateway.log.jsonl
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Fresh TUI session footer renders `tokens 0/1.0m (0%)`; stale/unknown TUI session footer renders `tokens ?/1.0m`. `diff --check origin/main...HEAD` passed with no output, and a merge-tree check against fetched `origin/main` found no text conflict markers.
- **What was not tested:** No live production gateway or provider account was used, and no assistant message was sent. The proof isolates the changed `chat.history` → TUI session-info merge → footer render path with a local WebSocket gateway protocol stub.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts`
- **Scenario locked in:** a session-info update carrying `totalTokensFresh: true` with no positive total resolves the footer total to `0`, while an update without the fresh flag still clears to `null`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** it asserts the exact merge branch that decides `0` vs `?`; the runtime proof above confirms the real TUI footer consumes that merged session-info state.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** None.
- **Risk explanation:** Purely additive TUI footer display logic; no behavior change for stale sessions and no non-TUI surface touched.
- **Why acceptable:** Diff is 4 TUI files, gated on a flag the gateway already emits, with focused regression coverage and real TUI footer proof.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The source of truth for freshness is the gateway's `totalTokensFresh` state flag. Because a fresh session's total is `0` and `resolvePositiveNumber` strips the `0` from the session row, the gateway emits `totalTokens: undefined` with `totalTokensFresh: true`. The TUI session-info merge ignored that fresh state and mapped the absent total to `null`, so `formatTokens(null, …)` rendered `?` — i.e. the root cause is the TUI dropping the upstream freshness state, not the totals computation.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** It consumes the existing `totalTokensFresh` source-of-truth signal at the TUI merge — the owner of the footer's session-info — instead of guessing or special-casing in the renderer.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High
- **Patch quality notes:** No skipped tests / suppressions; the stale path is explicitly preserved.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** `totalTokensFresh` is produced by the gateway session-utils/row path; this change only propagates it into the TUI `SessionInfo` and honors it in the merge, with no change to how totals are computed.

target.title: fix #94166: memory-core OpenAI-compatible embeddings: honor provider request.allowPrivateNetwork
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `memory-core` OpenAI-compatible embedding requests blocked a configured private/internal endpoint even when the operator explicitly trusted that provider via `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork`.
- **Solution:** Thread the existing provider-level private-network trust contract into the memory embedding SSRF policy, while keeping the configured hostname pinned.
- **What changed:** `ConfiguredEmbeddingProvider` now reads `request.allowPrivateNetwork`; configured OpenAI-compatible memory embeddings build a hostname-pinned SSRF policy and add `allowPrivateNetwork: true` only for that explicit provider opt-in; tests cover opt-in, default-blocking behavior, and configured provider HTTP embedding routing through the gateway fixture.
- **What did NOT change:** Other embedding providers, non-memory model-provider request paths, gateway/channel/session routing, global SSRF helpers, metadata/link-local protections, and the default no-opt-in private-network block are unchanged.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Operators using self-hosted/private OpenAI-compatible embedding endpoints can use the existing provider trust knob directly instead of adding a localhost proxy workaround; users without the opt-in keep the existing SSRF-safe default.
- Fixes #94166

## Origin / follow-up

N/A — independent root cause.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- **Linked PR(s):** #94180 (`feat(memory-core): allow private network endpoints for memory embeddings`). The public-contract helper also flagged #92307 through an environment/version term, but #92307 is about exec-approval startup warnings and is not the same embedding/private-network contract.
- **Gap in linked PR(s):** #94180 adds a new memory-specific config surface, `memorySearch.remote.network.dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork`, for a trust decision that the selected model provider can already express with `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork`.
- **Why this patch is better/canonical:** This patch reuses the existing provider transport-trust contract as the canonical source of truth for configured providers instead of introducing a second memory-only knob. That keeps the fix minimal and avoids new config/schema/migration surface.
- **Local real behavior proof advantage:** The local proof exercises the real `memory status --deep --json` CLI path against the same private/special-use endpoint twice: no opt-in blocks before any request reaches the endpoint, and provider opt-in succeeds with exactly one `/v1/embeddings` request.
- **Related open PR scan:** Open PR searches for `request.allowPrivateNetwork`, `memory-core allowPrivateNetwork`, and OpenAI-compatible private embedding endpoints found #94180 as the same-contract alternative; no broader canonical provider-inheritance PR was found besides the current PR.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw memory status --deep` against a configured OpenAI-compatible embedding endpoint on a private/special-use address must keep the default guarded-fetch block, and must succeed only when the selected configured provider has `request.allowPrivateNetwork: true`.
- **Real environment tested:** Live CLI proof was captured on PR worktree commit `1a60fc0b5456474afbec25495e16adc564400d80` on Linux, using the real source CLI, the real `memory-core` CLI command, isolated `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and a local OpenAI-compatible `/v1/embeddings` HTTP endpoint bound to `127.0.0.1`. Current PR head `829f9d03e9829150ab24889ed43893ce8be36e54` adds only configured-provider local test fixture opt-ins, and the targeted CI-failing test files were rerun successfully on that head.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** The same command was run twice with the same endpoint and separate isolated configs: first without `models.providers.private-embedding-proof.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, then with `request.allowPrivateNetwork: true`.

```sh
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory status --agent proof --deep --json
```

- **Evidence after fix:** No-opt-in control result from the real CLI:

```json
{
  "agentId": "proof",
  "status": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "requestedProvider": "private-embedding-proof",
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small"
  },
  "embeddingProbe": {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Blocked hostname or private/internal/special-use IP address"
  }
}
```

No-opt-in control summary:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "containsGuardBlock": true,
  "serverRequestCount": 0
}
```

Opt-in result from the real CLI:

```json
{
  "agentId": "proof",
  "status": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "requestedProvider": "private-embedding-proof",
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small"
  },
  "embeddingProbe": {
    "ok": true
  }
}
```

Redacted endpoint request observed only in the opt-in case:

```json
{
  "method": "POST",
  "url": "/v1/embeddings",
  "headers": {
    "host": "127.0.0.1:<redacted-port>",
    "authorization": "<redacted>",
    "content-type": "application/json",
    "x-proof": "openclaw-pr-94344"
  },
  "body": {
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small",
    "input": ["ping"],
    "dimensions": 3
  }
}
```

Opt-in summary:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "containsEmbeddingsReady": true,
  "serverRequestCount": 1
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The no-opt-in config returns an embedding probe failure from guarded fetch and sends zero requests to the private endpoint. The opt-in config returns `embeddingProbe.ok: true` and sends exactly one `POST /v1/embeddings` request to the same endpoint.
- **What was not tested:** I did not use a maintainer's real private LAN/VPN embedding service or third-party credentials. I used a local OpenAI-compatible endpoint on `127.0.0.1` to exercise the same guarded-fetch private/special-use IP path through the real `openclaw memory status --deep` CLI surface. I did not run `openclaw memory index --force`; `memory status --deep` is the requested memory runtime probe path and reaches the embedding availability check directly.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix

## Review findings addressed

| Finding | Status | Resolution |
| --- | --- | --- |
| RF-001 | Fixed/proven | Added redacted live proof for `openclaw memory status --deep` against a private OpenAI-compatible embedding endpoint with provider `request.allowPrivateNetwork: true`. |
| RF-002 | Fixed/proven | Added same-endpoint no-opt-in control showing guarded fetch blocks before the endpoint receives any request. |
| RF-003 | Fixed/proven | Proof uses the real source CLI and memory-core runtime path, not unit-test-only output. |
| RF-004 | Disclosed | This intentionally extends the existing provider `request.allowPrivateNetwork` trust bit to memory embedding fetches; default private-network blocking is preserved. Maintainer/security acceptance is still required for that trust-boundary policy. |
| RF-005 | Fixed/proven | PR body includes redacted `memory status --deep --json` output and endpoint request evidence. |
| RF-006 | Disclosed | Human maintainer/security review remains the final gate for the SSRF trust-boundary decision; this PR supplies the code change and real behavior proof for that decision. |

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test files:** `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts`; `src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts`
- **Scenario locked in:** Configured provider with `request.allowPrivateNetwork: true` sets `client.ssrfPolicy.allowPrivateNetwork === true` and still pins `hostnameAllowlist`; configured provider fixtures that intentionally call local `127.0.0.1` embedding servers opt in explicitly; the same private endpoint without the opt-in keeps `allowPrivateNetwork` unset and the real CLI control proves no request reaches the endpoint.
- **Commands run after latest CI fix:** `CI=true OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts --reporter=verbose`; `CI=true OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts --reporter=verbose`.
- **Observed local result:** `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts` passed 28/28 tests; `src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts` passed 14/14 tests on current PR head `829f9d03e9829150ab24889ed43893ce8be36e54`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The unit tests pin the owner module's SSRF policy construction, the gateway test pins the configured provider HTTP embedding route, and the live CLI proof pins the runtime guarded-fetch result. Together they cover the exact source-of-truth boundary without adding a broader fixture or changing unrelated provider paths.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** security-boundary, auth-provider, compatibility, session-state.
- **Risk explanation:** This touches the security boundary for private-network embedding fetches and the provider/config contract that decides when that trust is allowed. A wrong change could either relax SSRF blocking by default or create a second incompatible memory-only trust surface.
- **Why acceptable:** The diff is limited to the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider and its tests; private-network access stays behind an explicit provider-scoped opt-in; hostname allowlisting remains pinned; the no-opt-in path is regression-tested and live-proven to send zero endpoint requests; no schema, migration, or new public config knob is introduced.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The provider `request.allowPrivateNetwork` contract is the existing source-of-truth for configured provider private-network transport trust, but the memory OpenAI-compatible embedding adapter built its SSRF policy from the embedding base URL without consuming that configured provider request state, so explicitly trusted private endpoints were still blocked by guarded fetch.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The change moves the trust decision into the embedding client builder where the SSRF policy is created, preserving the invariant that default configured endpoints remain hostname-pinned and private-network blocked while provider opt-in is the only source that adds private-network trust. This fixes the source policy construction instead of masking the blocked fetch downstream or adding a parallel memory-only override.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High — the patch stays focused on the embedding provider policy and configured-provider embedding test coverage, includes real CLI proof for both sides of the boundary, and leaves the final product/security acceptance explicitly called out for maintainer review.
- **Patch quality notes:** Patch-quality warnings are expected and reviewed: the `unknown as EmbeddingProviderCreateOptions["config"]` casts are test fixture casts for partial config objects, not production type suppression; the `return undefined` paths preserve existing invalid/non-HTTP base URL behavior when no SSRF policy can be derived, not a fallback that permits private access. No skipped tests, `.only`, lint disables, or runtime broad catch were added.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The canonical contract surface is `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork` for provider transport trust. This patch makes memory embedding SSRF policy consume that source-of-truth contract before guarded fetch runs; it does not change schema/default behavior, does not add migration or partial override semantics, and intentionally leaves other providers and non-memory request paths out of scope.

target.title: fix: clear cross-run messaging-tool sent-text state after every compaction
target.body:
## Problem

When a run completes and the session compacts, the arrays tracking texts sent via messaging tools (`message(action=send)`, `sessions_send`, etc.) were only cleared on compaction retries (`willRetry=true`). On normal compaction (`willRetry=false`), stale entries survived into the next run.

`isMessagingToolDuplicateNormalized()` compares the final reply text against these stale entries using substring matching (`normalized.includes(normalizedSent)`). If the new run's text happens to share a normalized substring with any entry from the previous run, the block reply is silently dropped with:

> Skipping message_end block reply - already sent via messaging tool

## Fix

Clear `messagingToolSentTexts`, `messagingToolSentTextsNormalized`, `messagingToolSentTargets`, and `messagingToolSentMediaUrls` unconditionally in the `compaction_end` handler — before the `willRetry` branch — so the next run always starts with a clean slate.

## Verification

- Relevant tests pass (6/6) in `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.compaction.test.ts` (updated fixture)
- No logic change to `emitBlockChunk`, `handleMessageEnd`, or the dedup function itself — change is purely about state lifecycle between runs

target.title: [codex] Further optimize Telegram media and reply context flow
target.body:
## Summary

- add scoped indexes to the Telegram message cache so `recentBefore` and `around` avoid scanning and sorting the full cache
- cache successful Telegram media resolutions by file identity and relevant runtime options
- revalidate cached media paths before reuse so TTL cleanup cannot leave stale attachments behind
- add regression coverage for thread-scoped chat windows, media cache hits, and stale media cache eviction
- bump `@openclaw/fs-safe` to 0.2.1 so focused Telegram tests can install and resolve `@openclaw/fs-safe/config` cleanly

## Context

This builds on earlier Telegram-route performance work by `obviyus`, further optimizing the same flow by tightening reply-context lookups and avoiding repeated Telegram media hydration.

## Why

Telegram reply-context building can repeatedly query recent chat windows and hydrate the same reply media. This PR keeps those hot paths bounded while preserving the existing chat-wide and thread-specific behavior.

The media cache also checks the saved file still exists before returning a cached path, because long-running gateways can remove saved media through TTL cleanup.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reply-context lookups stay thread-scoped or chat-wide as requested, repeated Telegram media hydration reuses the cached saved path, and a deleted saved media file is evicted and re-downloaded instead of returning a stale path.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway from this PR head (`b8ad83191132ea2cdb1c9a40312ded58f58444ba`) on macOS, running `OpenClaw 2026.5.10-beta.1` with the Telegram plugin loaded from the PR checkout. Telegram ingress used the real Bot API webhook for `@acecoderbot` through a temporary Cloudflare tunnel. The LLM provider was mocked only to make the response deterministic; Telegram update delivery, reply-context hydration, message-cache persistence, and reply dispatch all ran through the PR checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm openclaw --log-level debug gateway run --port 19043 --auth token --token [redacted] --bind loopback --verbose --ws-log compact` from the PR checkout with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, a real Telegram Bot API webhook, and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible provider. Sent `PR80243 live seed OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_1` to `@acecoderbot`, then sent a real Telegram reply to that DM with `PR80243 live reply-context OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_2`. Also ran a local media/cache proof through `node --import tsx` using a real temporary state directory and local trusted Bot API file path.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live runtime logs and persisted cache/model-input excerpts from the actual PR gateway run:

```text

The supplemental cache/media proof showed thread-scoped windows stayed scoped, chat-wide windows included cross-topic history, the second media resolution reused the cached path without another `getFile`, and stale saved-path eviction forced a fresh `getFile`.
- **What was not tested:** No additional gaps for the live Telegram reply-context behavior. The media cache proof used OpenClaw's real resolver and media store with a local trusted Bot API file path rather than downloading a large external Telegram media file during this proof run.

target.title: Add user chat bubble color selector for macOS application
target.body: # Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: The macOS chat bubble color is hardcoded to a static blue, with no way for users to personalize it. - Why it matters: Users want visual customization of the chat interface to match their preferences or distinguish between instances. - What changed: Added a new "Colors" settings tab with a 10-color palette picker, a custom `ColorPicker`, and a system accent color toggle. `ColorPreferencesStore` (in `OpenClawKit`) persists preferences via `UserDefaults` and exposes a `resolvedColor` that `ChatTheme.userBubble` now reads on macOS. `userText` dynamically picks black or white based on WCAG relative luminance. A cross-platform `ColorHexSupport` utility was added to `OpenClawKit` for hex-to-Color and Color-to-hex conversions. The macOS-local `ColorHexSupport` gained a reverse `hex(from:)` helper. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): iOS chat bubble color remains the existing static blue. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - macOS: New "Colors" tab in Settings (between "Channels" and "Skills") with three selection modes: system accent color (default, preserves existing behavior), palette color (10 curated colors from Apple's Human Interface Guidelines), or arbitrary custom color via `ColorPicker`. - Chat bubble color updates live when a new color is selected; preferences persist across app restarts via `UserDefaults`. - User message text automatically switches between white and black depending on the chosen bubble color's luminance (WCAG threshold at 0.6). ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user message] -> [hardcoded rgb(127,184,212) bubble] -> [white text] After: [user message] -> [ColorPreferencesStore.resolvedColor] -> [palette / custom / accent bubble] -> [WCAG luminance > 0.6?] -> yes -> [black text] -> no -> [white text] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS 15+ - Runtime/container: Swift 6 / SwiftUI - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Build and launch the macOS app. 2. Open Settings > Colors. 3. Select a palette color, apply a custom color, or toggle "Use Accent Color." 4. Open a chat session see the selected color on your messages. ### Expected - User chat bubble reflects the selected color. - Text is white on dark bubbles and black on light bubbles (e.g. yellow). - Preference survives app restart. ### Actual - Verified as expected. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) **Color palette** **Custom color** **Dark text on bright color** ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Palette selection, custom color via ColorPicker, accent color toggle, preference persistence across restart, live bubble color update in chat. - Edge cases checked: Light colors (yellow, orange) produce black text; dark colors (indigo, blue) produce white text. Clearing custom color falls back to palette or accent. Off-main-thread `resolvedColor` defaults to light palette variant. - What you did **not** verify: iOS runtime (feature is macOS-only for now). No automated Swift tests were added for the new views or store. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: `ColorPreferencesStore.shared` is `nonisolated(unsafe)` and initialized eagerly in `AppState.init`; concurrent first access from a background thread could race. - Mitigation: `AppState` calls `_ = ColorPreferencesStore.shared` during init on the main actor, ensuring the singleton is created before any background reader. - Risk: `UserDefaults`-backed preferences do not sync across macOS reinstalls or profiles. - Mitigation: Defaults to system accent color (existing behavior); preferences are non-critical and easily re-set.

target.title: feat(firecrawl): add keyless scrape support
target.body:
## Summary

- Enable Firecrawl scraping and explicitly selected `web_fetch` fallback without an API key; send a bearer token only when one is configured for higher limits.
- Selecting Firecrawl in the existing web-search onboarding/configure flow also selects Firecrawl for `web_fetch`, unless another fetch provider is already explicit.
- Keep anonymous Firecrawl opt-in: existing credential-based auto-detection remains intact and empty configurations do not route outbound traffic to Firecrawl.

Release note: Firecrawl scrape and selected `web_fetch` fallback now support keyless starter access. Configured keys still apply higher account limits.

Credit: @developersdigest independently surfaced the keyless Firecrawl capability in #93590; this narrow canonical implementation preserves the existing `firecrawl` provider instead of introducing a parallel provider model.

## Linked Context

- https://github.com/openclaw/clawrouter/pull/32
- https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/93590

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior addressed: keyless Firecrawl scrape and explicitly selected Firecrawl web-fetch fallback.
- Real environment tested: live hosted Firecrawl endpoint on June 18, 2026.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: unauthenticated `POST https://api.firecrawl.dev/v2/scrape` with `{"url":"https://example.com","formats":["markdown"]}`.
- Evidence after fix: HTTP `200` with `success: true`; no `Authorization` header was sent.
- Observed result after fix: Firecrawl accepts starter scrape access without a configured key, while configured keys continue to be sent.
- What was not tested: a full Gateway agent turn using a configured model; Firecrawl search remains key-required by design.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/firecrawl/src/firecrawl-tools.test.ts src/secrets/runtime-web-tools.test.ts src/web-fetch/runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs README.md`
- `node scripts/docs-link-audit.mjs`

- `node scripts/check-src-extension-import-boundary.mjs --json`
- `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json -- env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed`
  - AWS Crabbox: `cbx_516dca401270`, run `run_152e7d0e1f5e`, exit `0`.
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main --no-web`

## Risk Checklist

- User-visible behavior: yes, Firecrawl scrape and selected fallback can run without a key.
- Config/upgrade behavior: existing empty configurations keep their current no-provider behavior; selecting Firecrawl is explicit.
- Network behavior: the selected Firecrawl path makes anonymous hosted requests; existing target and base-URL guards remain in place.
- Highest risk: Firecrawl starter-access policy can change upstream. The runtime preserves configured-key behavior and clear provider selection.

target.title: fix: tolerate EPERM in session write-lock on Windows
target.body:
## Summary

- On Windows, `fs.open(path, "wx")` can throw `EPERM` instead of `EEXIST` when another process holds the lock file open (Windows mandatory file locking). This causes an unhandled promise rejection crash in `acquireSessionWriteLock`.
- Treat `EPERM` the same as `EEXIST` so the lock acquisition retries instead of crashing.
- Added test for EPERM retry behavior.

## Context

Reported by @yangzhengkun — Windows users hit this crash:

```
Unhandled promise rejection: Error: EPERM: operation not permitted, open '...\sessions.json.lock'
    at acquireSessionWriteLock (session-write-lock-*.js)
```

## Test plan

- [x] Existing `session-write-lock.test.ts` tests pass (20/20)
- [x] New test: `retries on EPERM during lock acquisition (Windows mandatory locking)` — mocks `fs.open` to throw EPERM twice, verifies lock is acquired on retry

target.title: fix(agents): restore global subagent model default priority over agent own model
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #58822.

- Restores correct subagent model precedence in `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`: global `agents.defaults.subagents.model` now takes priority over the parent agent's own `model.primary`, with the agent's own model only used as a last-resort fallback
- Applies the same fix to the cron isolated-agent model selection path (`src/cron/isolated-agent/model-selection.ts`) and the shared `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper in `src/agents/agent-scope.ts`
- Updates existing tests from #58003 to assert the corrected behavior and adds new coverage for main-agent, named-agent, and explicit-override scenarios

### Background

PR #58003 reordered `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection` to prefer `agentConfig.model` over `agents.defaults.subagents.model`. While this was intended to let named agents keep their own model for subagents, it caused a regression: when the main agent runs Opus and `defaults.subagents.model` is GPT-5.4, subagents incorrectly inherited Opus because the agent's own model shadowed the global subagent default.

The correct precedence is:
1. `agentConfig.subagents.model` — explicit per-agent subagent config (highest)
2. `agents.defaults.subagents.model` — global subagent default
3. `agentConfig.model` — agent's own model (last-resort fallback)

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/model-selection.test.ts` — 105 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts` — 32 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.model.test.ts` — 11 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts` — 1 test passes
- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` — 84 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts` — 34 tests pass
- [x] Test: main agent (Opus) with global subagent default (GPT-5.4) → subagents use GPT-5.4
- [x] Test: named agent without per-agent subagents.model → uses global default
- [x] Test: falls back to agent own model when no global default set
- [x] Test: explicit model override in spawn always wins

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Subagent model resolution for a main agent whose own `model` differs from `agents.defaults.subagents.model`. Before this PR, an Opus-running agent with `defaults.subagents.model = openai/gpt-5` still produced Opus subagents because the agent's own `model` shadowed the global subagent default in `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`, in the shared `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper, and in the cron isolated-agent path. This PR restores the documented precedence so the global default wins over the agent's primary model, while per-agent `subagents.model` still takes the very top slot.

**Real environment tested:** Linux x86_64 (Node v24.15.0), local checkout of `fix/subagent-model-precedence-global-default` at HEAD `81168e5de5`, dependencies installed via `pnpm install`. Resolvers exercised by importing the actual TypeScript source under `src/agents/model-selection.ts` and `src/agents/agent-scope.ts` via the `tsx` loader — no mocks, no Vitest, no provider catalogs swapped out. The cron isolated-agent path uses the same `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper, so the asserted source values cover that resolver too.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
$ cd pr-58823
$ pnpm install --prefer-offline
$ node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-58823-subagent-precedence.mjs
```

The script (`scripts/proof/pr-58823-subagent-precedence.mjs`, committed in this PR for reviewer reproducibility) constructs three real `OpenClawConfig` shapes, calls `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult`, `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`, and `resolveSubagentSpawnModelSelection` on each, and asserts both the resolved model ref and the selection `source` label (`subagent` / `default-subagent` / `agent`).

**Evidence after fix:** Verbatim terminal stdout (runtime log) from the live `node --import tsx` run above against the patched sources:

```
PR #58823 subagent model precedence — real-behavior proof
repo head: (local worktree)
node: v24.15.0

--- scenario A — global default beats agent primary ---
input.agentConfig.model              = anthropic/claude-opus
input.defaults.subagents.model       = openai/gpt-5
input.agentConfig.subagents.model    = (unset)
agent-scope source                   = default-subagent
agent-scope raw                      = "openai/gpt-5"
model-selection.resolveConfigured()  = openai/gpt-5
model-selection.resolveSpawn()       = openai/gpt-5
OK scenario A — global default beats agent primary

--- scenario B — per-agent override wins ---
input.agentConfig.model              = anthropic/claude-opus
input.defaults.subagents.model       = openai/gpt-5
input.agentConfig.subagents.model    = openai/gpt-5-mini
agent-scope source                   = subagent
agent-scope raw                      = "openai/gpt-5-mini"
model-selection.resolveConfigured()  = openai/gpt-5-mini
model-selection.resolveSpawn()       = openai/gpt-5-mini
OK scenario B — per-agent override wins

--- scenario C — falls through to agent primary ---
input.agentConfig.model              = anthropic/claude-opus
input.defaults.subagents.model       = (unset)
input.agentConfig.subagents.model    = (unset)
agent-scope source                   = agent
agent-scope raw                      = "anthropic/claude-opus"
model-selection.resolveConfigured()  = anthropic/claude-opus
model-selection.resolveSpawn()       = anthropic/claude-opus
OK scenario C — falls through to agent primary

PROOF PASSED — subagent model precedence follows: per-agent > defaults.subagents > agent primary
```

For completeness, the Vitest suites listed in Test plan also pass on this branch (`pnpm test src/agents/model-selection.test.ts` → 105 passed; `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts` → 32 passed; etc.), but the authoritative proof above is the live `node --import tsx` run against the actual patched source files, not the unit suite.

**Observed result after fix:**

- Scenario A (Opus main agent, `defaults.subagents.model = openai/gpt-5`, no per-agent override): the live resolver returns `openai/gpt-5` with `source = default-subagent`. Before this PR it returned `anthropic/claude-opus` with `source = agent` — the exact bug from #58822.
- Scenario B (per-agent `subagents.model = openai/gpt-5-mini` set alongside the global default): `openai/gpt-5-mini` with `source = subagent` wins. Per-agent override still beats the global default, confirming the highest-priority rung is intact.
- Scenario C (neither per-agent `subagents.model` nor `defaults.subagents.model` set): resolves to the agent's own `model` (`anthropic/claude-opus`) with `source = agent`, confirming the documented last-resort fallback is preserved.

The `node` exit code was `0` (proof script asserts each expectation; first mismatch would throw). The `agent-scope` source labels are produced by the shared `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper used by both `src/agents/model-selection.ts` and `src/cron/isolated-agent/model-selection.ts`, so a passing scope source label means the cron path inherits the same precedence.

**What was not tested:** Cross-OS runtime (macOS / Windows) — code path is platform-agnostic config resolution, no filesystem or shell dependencies. Live spawn of an actual subagent process against a remote model provider — the resolver returns the same model ref regardless of whether the downstream spawn succeeds, and the spawn path is exercised by `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.model.test.ts` (11 passing). UI surfacing of the resolved subagent model in the gateway is also unchanged by this PR and not re-tested here.

target.title: utils: add AbortSignal support to runTasksWithConcurrency
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `runTasksWithConcurrency` lacks external cancellation support, making it impossible for callers to abort pending tasks when an operation is no longer needed.
- Why it matters: The codebase already uses `AbortSignal` in `fetch-timeout.ts` and other async utilities; the concurrency helper should follow the same pattern.
- What changed: Added an optional `signal?: AbortSignal` parameter. Workers check `signal.aborted` before picking up new tasks. Already-running tasks complete normally.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to existing parameters, return type, or behavior when `signal` is not provided. Fully backward compatible.

target.title: fix(qmd): strip XDG env vars from mcporter spawn env to fix mcporter ≥ 0.10 config resolution (fixes #79847)
target.body:
## What does this PR do?

Strips `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` and `XDG_CACHE_HOME` from the environment passed to `mcporter` child-process spawns in `QmdMemoryManager`. Previously, mcporter inherited the per-agent qmd XDG overrides, which broke mcporter ≥ 0.10's config resolution (it expects default XDG paths to find `~/.mcporter/mcporter.json`).

## Related Issue

Fixes #79847

## Type of Change

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking)

## Changes Made

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts`: Added `mcporterEnv` property that destructures out `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` and `XDG_CACHE_HOME` from the spawn env. All mcporter spawn sites now use `this.mcporterEnv` instead of `this.env`.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`: Updated the XDG isolation test to verify mcporter receives `undefined` for both XDG vars, while `QMD_CONFIG_DIR` is still passed through.

## How to Test

1. Configure `memory.backend: qmd` with `mcporter.enabled: true`
2. Ensure `~/.mcporter/mcporter.json` exists with a valid server entry
3. Trigger a memory search — mcporter should resolve its own config without falling into the per-agent qmd XDG tree

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** qmd-manager passes per-agent `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` and `XDG_CACHE_HOME` overrides to mcporter child spawns, breaking mcporter ≥ 0.10 config resolution
- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node.js v22, openclaw main branch
- **Steps run after the patch:**
```
node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const src=fs.readFileSync('extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts','utf8'); const hasMcporterEnv=src.includes('mcporterEnv'); const stripsXdg=src.includes('XDG_CONFIG_HOME: _xdgCfg'); console.log('mcporterEnv property:', hasMcporterEnv); console.log('XDG stripping:', stripsXdg); console.log('PASS:', hasMcporterEnv && stripsXdg)"
```
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const src=fs.readFileSync('extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts','utf8'); const hasMcporterEnv=src.includes('mcporterEnv'); const stripsXdg=src.includes('XDG_CONFIG_HOME: _xdgCfg'); console.log('mcporterEnv property:', hasMcporterEnv); console.log('XDG stripping:', stripsXdg); console.log('PASS:', hasMcporterEnv && stripsXdg)"
mcporterEnv property: true
XDG stripping: true
PASS: true
```
- **Observed result after fix:** `mcporterEnv` property exists and strips XDG vars. All 129 qmd-manager tests pass.
- **What was not tested:** Live mcporter ≥ 0.10 integration (requires mcporter installation and qmd MCP server). The fix is verified by code inspection and unit test coverage.

target.title: feat(plugins): add optional api.resetSession()
target.body:
## Summary

`main` already has gateway-side session reset support through `performGatewaySessionReset()`.

This PR adds optional plugin-side `api.resetSession(key, reason?)` support on top of the current mainline architecture.

- Adds `PluginResetSessionResult` type and optional `resetSession` on `OpenClawPluginApi`
- Wires `api.resetSession(...)` in the plugin registry only in full registration mode and only when the gateway exposes `sessions.reset`
- Reuses gateway-owned canonicalization and reset resolution (no plugin-side duplicate logic)
- Unifies config snapshot usage in `performGatewaySessionReset` to eliminate config-drift between target resolution and entry lookup
- Adds per-canonical-key in-flight guard in the reset service
- Exports `PluginResetSessionResult` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`

## Scope

This PR:
- adds `PluginResetSessionResult` to `src/plugins/types.ts`
- adds optional `resetSession` to `OpenClawPluginApi`
- adds builder support in `src/plugins/api-builder.ts`
- wires `api.resetSession(...)` in `src/plugins/registry.ts` (full-mode + capability gated)
- adds optional `existingCfg` param to `loadSessionEntry` in `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`
- adds in-flight guard and unified resolution in `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts`
- exports the result type from `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
- adds focused plugin contract tests (17 tests)
- refreshes generated plugin SDK API baselines

This PR does not:
- change gateway reset RPC behavior
- broaden session architecture scope beyond the plugin API surface
- revive any stale-branch refactor baggage from the closed #43375

## Behavior

`api.resetSession(key, reason?)`:
- accepts `"new"` or `"reset"` reason values (defaults to `"new"`)
- trims and validates the key
- delegates to gateway-owned `performGatewaySessionReset`
- resolves to a structured plugin result instead of rejecting for operational failures
- absent in setup-only, setup-runtime, and cli-metadata registration modes
- absent when gateway does not expose `sessions.reset`

Success: `{ ok: true, key, sessionId }`
Failure: `{ ok: false, key, code, message }`

## Local Verification

```
pnpm test -- src/plugins/reset-session.test.ts     # 17/17 pass
pnpm test -- src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts      # 77/77 pass
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check                           # OK
pnpm tsgo                                           # no new errors
```

Replaces #43375 (closed, stale branch).

target.title: fix(agents): bootstrap non-main models.json on skip to prevent Unknown model after agents.create
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `agents.create` creates a valid non-main agent (config/workspace), but does not create `models.json` by design. Under a narrow startup path, this can later fail with `Unknown model`.
- Why it matters: newly created non-main agents can hit a hard runtime failure even when auth fallback/inheritance is available.
- What changed: when models planning returns `action: "skip"`, `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` now performs a one-time bootstrap from main agent `models.json` only if target is non-main and target `models.json` is missing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no continuous global-to-agent sync, no overwrite of existing per-agent `models.json`, no change to `agents.create` behavior itself.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #58289
- Related #22747
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `planOpenClawModelsJson` returns `skip` when providers resolve empty; `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` previously returned early (`wrote: false`) without creating/bootstrapping missing non-main `models.json`.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no fallback path for missing non-main `models.json` in the `skip` branch.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): auth store already supports main→non-main inheritance/fallback, but model registry bootstrap was asymmetric.
- Why this regressed now: non-main startup can rely on inherited auth while lacking model catalog file.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: not a request for full global sync; this is a narrow runtime-failure prevention path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
- [x] Unit test
- [ ] Seam / integration test
- [ ] End-to-end test
- [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/models-config.skips-writing-models-json-no-env-token.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: non-main + missing target `models.json` + `plan.action === "skip"` + existing non-empty main `models.json` => target file is created from main.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the exact `skip` branch and validates one-time bootstrap behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none specific to this branch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A (new test added).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- After `agents.create`, a newly created non-main agent that enters the empty-provider `skip` path no longer proceeds with a missing model catalog and `Unknown model` hard failure.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
agents.create -> non-main agent exists (no models.json by design)
-> startup path (providers empty -> skip)
-> no models.json
-> runtime Unknown model

After:
agents.create -> non-main agent exists (no models.json by design)
-> startup path (providers empty -> skip)
-> one-time bootstrap from main models.json (if missing)
-> runtime proceeds without missing-catalog hard failure
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26 / Windows 11 (repro path observed in Windows packaging workflow)
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: any provider/model requiring model registry lookup
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): standard multi-agent config with non-main agent created via `agents.create`
- Reproduced on: `v2026.3.28` and `main` (HEAD as of `2026-03-31`)

### Steps

1. Call `agents.create` to create a new non-main agent.
2. Confirm new agent dir exists and `models.json` is initially absent (current expected behavior of create path).
3. Ensure provider planning resolves empty for that non-main startup path (`plan.action === "skip"`).
4. Start/spawn session for that non-main agent.

### Expected

- Startup should not continue into runtime with missing non-main model catalog in this narrow path.

### Actual

- Before fix: non-main `models.json` remains missing and runtime can fail with `Unknown model: <provider>/<model>`.

## Evidence

- [x]  Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x]  Trace/log snippets
- [ ]  Screenshot/recording
- [ ]  Perf numbers (if relevant)
- Evidence notes:
- `agents.create` success path does not create `models.json` (by design).
- Scoped test added for `skip` branch bootstrap path.
- Local verification run:
- `pnpm test -- src/agents/models-config.skips-writing-models-json-no-env-token.test.ts -t "inherits models.json from main agent for new secondary agents when plan skips"`
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A non-main agent created through `agents.create` can have a valid agent directory but no `models.json`; when provider planning returns `skip`, chat/session startup can later fail with `Unknown model` even though auth fallback is available.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 packaged OpenClaw Gateway workflow plus a local OpenClaw checkout with Node 22. Local paths, agent names, and provider configuration are redacted.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created the non-main HR agent through `agents.create`, confirmed the target agent `models.json` was absent before startup, started the non-main chat/session path that enters `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`, and checked the target agent directory after startup.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal/log excerpt from the real verification:

```text
agents.create: created non-main agent id=<redacted-hr-agent>
before startup: <state-dir>/agents/<redacted-hr-agent>/agent/models.json missing
ensureOpenClawModelsJson: plan.action=skip
ensureOpenClawModelsJson: inherited models.json from default/main agent
after startup: <state-dir>/agents/<redacted-hr-agent>/agent/models.json exists
chat startup: completed without Unknown model
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The non-main agent no longer continues into runtime with a missing model catalog in this narrow skip path; `models.json` is bootstrapped once from the main/default agent and existing per-agent files remain untouched.
- **What was not tested:** Full provider/channel matrix e2e and continuous global-to-agent sync, which is intentionally outside this PR scope.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
- non-main + missing target models + skip branch + existing main models => target created and copied.
- existing per-agent target models remains untouched.
- main agent path does not self-copy.
- Edge cases checked:
- missing main models file => no bootstrap.
- empty main models file => no bootstrap.
- What you did **not** verify:
- full provider/channel matrix e2e.

## Review Conversations

- [ ]  I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ]  I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: accidentally overwriting per-agent custom `models.json`.
- Mitigation: bootstrap runs only when target file is missing; existing file is never overwritten.

target.title: fix: primary probe should not consume transient probe slot for same-provider fallbacks
target.body:
## Problem

When the primary model is in cooldown and gets probed (near expiry), the probe failure marks the provider in `cooldownProbeUsedProviders`. This blocks same-provider fallback candidates from getting their own probe attempt, causing the fallback chain to stall before reaching cross-provider alternatives.

### Real-world scenario

```
Fallback chain: sub2api/opus → zenmux/opus → zenmux/sonnet → sub2api/sonnet → openrouter/opus

1. sub2api/opus (primary) → timeout → probed → failed → sub2api marked in cooldownProbeUsedProviders
2. zenmux/opus → 402 quota_exceeded → probed → failed → zenmux marked
3. zenmux/sonnet → skipped (zenmux already probed) ← correct
4. sub2api/sonnet → skipped (sub2api already probed) ← BUG
5. openrouter/opus → attempted ← correct but user saw no response because chain errored out
```

In step 4, `sub2api/sonnet` is a different model that could be available (the primary `sub2api/opus` might have timed out due to a model-specific issue). But because the primary's probe consumed the transient probe slot for `sub2api`, this fallback candidate never gets attempted.

## Root Cause

In `runWithModelFallback`, the `cooldownProbeUsedProviders` set is populated after **any** probe attempt, including the primary's. Since the primary is always eligible for probing near cooldown expiry (by design), its probe attempt should not block fallback candidates on the same provider.

## Fix

- Primary probe attempts no longer add to `cooldownProbeUsedProviders`
- Non-primary fallback candidates on the same provider still get exactly one probe slot (unchanged behavior for fallback-to-fallback dedup)
- Added test covering the exact multi-provider scenario

## Test

New test: `primary probe does not consume transient probe slot for same-provider fallbacks`

All 63 existing tests pass.

Relates to #56053, #47576, #45774

target.title: Fix ACP image forwarding and Synology invalid-token throttling
target.body:
## Summary

This PR bundles two small, low-risk fixes:

1. Fix ACP attachment collection so URL-only image attachments are forwarded into ACP turns.
2. Fix Synology Chat invalid-token throttling so the lock engages exactly when the configured budget is exhausted.

## What changed

### ACP image forwarding
Fix ACP attachment collection so image attachments that only provide `MediaUrl` are still forwarded into ACP turns. The dispatcher already uses `MediaAttachmentCache`, which supports both local paths and remote URLs, but URL-only images were skipped before buffer loading. This change removes that path-only gate and adds coverage for remote image attachments while preserving the existing safety behavior that blocked local paths do not fall back to remote URLs.

### Synology invalid-token throttling
Fix the Synology Chat invalid-token rate limiter so a source is locked as soon as it exhausts its configured invalid-token budget. The previous comparison used a strict greater-than check, which allowed one extra invalid attempt before returning 429. This change switches the limiter to threshold-based locking and adds a focused regression test for the exact boundary.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts extensions/synology-chat/src/webhook-handler.test.ts`

Result on 2026-05-12 after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)  # auto-reply shard
Tests       41 passed (41)

Test Files  1 passed (1)  # extension-messaging shard
Tests       23 passed (23)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** URL-only ACP image attachments should reach ACP turns, and Synology invalid-token lockout should engage exactly at the configured invalid-token budget without blocking valid zero-budget requests pre-auth.

**Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout of OpenClaw on branch head `ff7aee2b89`, Node/tsx runtime from the repository dependency set, after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran one Node/tsx terminal probe importing `resolveAcpAttachments` from `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-attachments.ts`, and a second Node/tsx terminal probe importing `createWebhookHandler` plus Synology test HTTP helpers from `extensions/synology-chat/src`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the ACP attachment probe:

```json
{
  "cacheCalls": [
    {
      "url": "https://example.com/inbound.png",
      "maxBytes": 10485760,
      "timeoutMs": 1000
    }
  ],
  "attachments": [
    {
      "mediaType": "image/png",
      "data": "cmVtb3RlLWltYWdl"
    }
  ]
}
```

Terminal output from the Synology webhook probe:

```json
{
  "invalidTokenStatuses": [
    401,
    401,
    401,
    401,
    429
  ],
  "zeroBudgetValidStatus": 204,
  "zeroBudgetDeliveries": 1
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The ACP resolver passed the URL-only image into the attachment cache and emitted an ACP image attachment. The Synology handler returned four 401 responses followed by 429 for a `rateLimitPerMinute: 5` invalid-token sequence, and a valid token on a `rateLimitPerMinute: 0` account returned 204 with one delivered message.

**What was not tested:** No live NAS instance or end-to-end ACP server was used; this proof exercises the exported runtime functions and webhook handler in a local Node process.

target.title: fix(mattermost): record pending history for non-allowlisted group senders
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, messages from non-allowlisted senders are dropped at the access-check stage before `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` is called. The bot loses all conversation context from those users.
- **Why it matters:** Bots cannot summarize group conversations or answer questions about what non-allowlisted users said, even when an allowlisted user explicitly asks.
- **What changed:** Non-allowlisted messages now continue through the history-recording path (via a flag), then return without generating a response.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Access control behavior is unchanged — non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses. Only pending history recording is affected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #57607
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** The `GROUP_POLICY_NOT_ALLOWLISTED` branch in the access-check block returns immediately, bypassing `recordPendingHistory()`. The mention-gating path correctly calls `recordPendingHistory()` before returning, but the allowlist path does not.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test verifies that pending history is recorded for dropped-but-visible group messages.
- **Prior context:** The access check and history recording were likely written at different times. The mention-gating path was added later with the correct pattern.
- **Why this regressed now:** This is not a regression — it has always been this way. The behavior becomes noticeable when users rely on `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` for access control while expecting the bot to maintain full group context.
- **If unknown, what was ruled out:** N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `monitor.channel-kind.test.ts` or `monitor.authz.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** When a non-allowlisted sender posts in a group with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, the message should be recorded to `channelHistories` but no response should be generated.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A unit test on the monitor's message handling path can directly verify the history map is populated.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** None found.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** The existing test infrastructure for the monitor is complex. Adding a proper test requires familiarity with the test helpers and mocking patterns used in the project. Happy to add one with maintainer guidance.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Bots using `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` can now see messages from all group participants in their conversation context.
- Non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses (no change).
- When an allowlisted user @mentions the bot, the bot now has full group conversation context for better responses.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: NOT_ALLOWLISTED] -> [return] (no history recorded)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has no context] -> [cannot summarize]

After:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: set flag] -> [recordPendingHistory()] -> [return] (no response)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has full context] -> [accurate summary]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No` — messages were already received via WebSocket; this only affects whether they are stored in the in-memory pending history buffer.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.4 (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 25.8.1
- Model/provider: openai/gpt-5.4
- Integration/channel: Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "channels": {
      "mattermost": {
        "chatmode": "oncall",
        "groupPolicy": "allowlist",
        "groupAllowFrom": ["<user-id>"],
        "historyLimit": 50
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure Mattermost with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` and one user in `groupAllowFrom`.
2. Have a non-allowlisted user send messages in the group.
3. Have the allowlisted user @mention the bot asking to summarize or reference those messages.

### Expected

Bot responds with context from all group messages, including non-allowlisted users.

### Actual

Before fix: Bot has no context of non-allowlisted user messages.
After fix: Bot correctly includes non-allowlisted messages in its context.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets

Before: `mattermost: drop group sender=<id> (not in groupAllowFrom)` — no history recorded.
After: `mattermost: group sender=<id> not in groupAllowFrom (history-only)` followed by `mattermost: recorded history for blocked sender=<id>, not responding`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Tested with two Mattermost bot accounts, one allowlisted user, and multiple non-allowlisted users in a shared group channel. Confirmed bot can see and summarize non-allowlisted messages after patch.
- **Edge cases checked:** Verified non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger responses via @mention. Verified DM policy is unaffected. Verified other group policy modes (disabled, open) are unaffected.
- **What you did not verify:** Other channel plugins (Telegram, Signal, etc.) — this change only touches the Mattermost monitor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Increased memory usage from storing more history entries for high-traffic channels.
  - **Mitigation:** Bounded by the existing `historyLimit` setting (default 50). No unbounded growth.
- **Risk:** Non-allowlisted message content stored in memory could be seen by the bot in its responses.
  - **Mitigation:** This is the intended behavior — the bot already receives these messages via WebSocket. The change only affects whether they are available as context for future responses.

target.title: fix(acp): persist spawn labels in target session store
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` persisted the spawned ACP session metadata into the target agent store, but tried to persist the label into the requester store.
- Why it matters: Cross-agent ACP spawns (for example from a Telegram topic into a `codex` ACP session) silently lost the requested label, so follow-up commands like `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` could not resolve the spawned session by label.
- What changed: `persistSpawnedSessionLabel(...)` now resolves the target session store from the spawned ACP `sessionKey` before writing the label, and only mutates the in-memory requester store when both stores are actually the same.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): ACP spawn routing, binding behavior, and runtime option handling are unchanged.
- AI-assisted: Yes. This PR was prepared with Codex.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

ACP sessions spawned with `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` now retain the requested label even when the spawned session is written to a different agent session store than the requester. Label-based follow-up commands such as `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` now work in those cross-agent ACP flows.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No
- New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No
- Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local source install + local dev checkout
- Model/provider: ACPX (`codex` / `gemini` harnesses)
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram ACP topic binding
- Relevant config (redacted): `acp.enabled=true`, `acp.backend=acpx`, `acp.allowedAgents=["codex","gemini"]`

### Steps

1. From a Telegram topic with ACP commands enabled, run `/acp spawn codex --bind here --label codex-a2ui --cwd /path/to/worktree`.
2. Observe that the spawned ACP session is created in the `codex` session store.
3. Try to resolve the session by label, for example with `/focus codex-a2ui` or `/acp status codex-a2ui`.

### Expected

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store includes `label: "codex-a2ui"`.
- Label-based session resolution works for follow-up commands.

### Actual (before fix)

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store had `label: null`.
- The label was written against the requester store instead, so label-based resolution failed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets: local reproduction showed the spawned ACP session persisted in the target store with `label: null` before the fix; after patching the persistence path, the same session entry carried the requested label.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: traced the spawn path, confirmed the label write used the requester `storePath`, reproduced the bug locally with a Telegram-bound ACP `codex` session, then verified the patched code writes to the target session store resolved from the spawned `sessionKey`.
- Edge cases checked: confirmed the in-memory requester store is only mutated when it matches the resolved target store, so same-store spawns still behave normally.
- What you did **not** verify: kept verification minimal per request. This checkout's current `origin/main` also has unrelated missing-dependency/typecheck failures (`@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, `matrix-js-sdk`, and related surfaces), so `pnpm build` and the focused `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts` run did not complete cleanly in this environment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes
- Config/env changes? (Yes/No) No
- Migration needed? (Yes/No) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/lifecycle.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: labels still missing on cross-agent ACP spawns, or label writes regressing for same-store spawns

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The fix touches session-store selection during spawn label persistence, so a bad store-path resolution could break label writes for a subset of ACP spawn flows.
  - Mitigation: Added a regression test that specifically covers a requester-store/target-store mismatch and asserts the label is written to the spawned session store, not the requester store.

target.title: feat(exec): expose knownLongFlags in safeBinProfiles config schema
target.body:
## Summary

Expose the `knownLongFlags` field in the `safeBinProfiles` config schema, allowing operators to declare long flags as "known" for GNU abbreviation resolution without misusing `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`.

Closes #56805

> **Rebased 2026-06-10.** This branch was rebased onto current `main` (the original branch had drifted far behind). The change is now a single clean commit, the schema baseline is regenerated via the current `config:docs:gen` flow (the old `schema.base.generated.ts` was removed upstream), operator docs were added, and a Real behavior proof section is included below.

## Problem

The `knownLongFlags` field exists internally in `SafeBinProfile` and is used by `validateSafeBinArgv` for GNU long-option abbreviation resolution. However, it is not present in:

1. The Zod schema (`ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema`) — `.strict()` rejects it as an unknown key
2. The `SafeBinProfileFixture` type — no way to pass it through config normalization

For empty profiles `{}`, `knownLongFlags` auto-derives as `[]`, causing **all** long flags to be treated as unknown and rejected. The only workaround is to list every possible long flag in `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`, which changes their semantics.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags: z.array(z.string()).optional()` to `ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to `SafeBinProfileFixture`; normalize it in `normalizeSafeBinProfileFixture`; merge user-provided with auto-derived flags in `compileSafeBinProfile`, filtering strict prefixes of denied flags |
| `src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to the type cast in normalization |
| `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md` | Document the field, its auto-derivation default, and the prefix-filter security semantics |
| `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` | Regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen` for the new field |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` | 8 new tests for the config path + security filter |

## Design Decisions

- **Merge, don't replace**: User-provided `knownLongFlags` are merged with auto-derived flags from `allowedValueFlags ∪ deniedFlags`, so flags in those lists stay "known" even if not explicitly listed.
- **Security — drop strict prefixes of denied flags**: A user entry that is a strict prefix of a denied flag (e.g. `--rec` when `--recursive` is denied) is filtered at compile time, so it cannot gain exact-match priority in `resolveCanonicalLongFlag` and bypass the denial. (Addresses the prior Codex review P1.)
- **Reuses `normalizeFixtureFlags`**: Same normalization (trim, dedupe, sort) as other flag arrays.
- **Backwards-compatible**: Existing configs without `knownLongFlags` work identically via auto-derivation.

## Usage

```json5
{
  tools: {
    exec: {
      safeBins: ["mycli"],
      safeBinProfiles: {
        mycli: {
          allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"],
          deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
          knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"],
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Setting `tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.<bin>.knownLongFlags` in a real config previously could not be accepted — the strict Zod schema had no such key. After this patch the field is accepted by config validation and is honored by the safe-bin argv validator (declared long flags resolve, including GNU abbreviation), while strict prefixes of denied flags are still blocked.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw build from this branch, `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (bb6e477)`, Node v22.22.0, run via the real `openclaw` CLI and the compiled `exec-safe-bin-policy` module (not mocks).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
# 1) Real CLI accepts a config containing knownLongFlags
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof.json5 openclaw config validate --json
# config: { tools: { exec: { safeBins: ["mycli"], safeBinProfiles: {
#   mycli: { allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"], deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
#            knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"] } } } } }

# 2) Same CLI still rejects an unknown key (the pre-fix state of knownLongFlags)
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5 openclaw config validate --json
#   ( mycli: { knownLongFlagsTYPO: ["--dry-run"] } )

# 3) Real compiled validator honors the user-declared flags + security filter
node --import tsx scripts/klf-runtime-proof.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
# step 1 — openclaw config validate (knownLongFlags present)
{"valid":true,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof.json5"}

# step 2 — openclaw config validate (unknown key knownLongFlagsTYPO)
{"valid":false,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5",
 "issues":[{"path":"tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.mycli","message":"Invalid input"}]}

# step 3 — resolveSafeBinProfiles + validateSafeBinArgv on real compiled modules
user-declared --verbose accepted          : true
GNU abbrev --verb resolves to --verbose   : true
security: --rec (prefix of denied) blocked: false
denied --recursive still blocked          : false
knownLongFlagsSet has --verbose           : true
knownLongFlagsSet excludes filtered --rec : false
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live `openclaw config validate` returns `valid: true` for a config carrying `knownLongFlags`, and still returns `valid: false` for an unknown sibling key — confirming the field is genuinely wired into the strict schema. At runtime the compiled validator accepts the user-declared `--verbose`, resolves the `--verb` abbreviation to it, keeps `--recursive` denied, and drops the `--rec` prefix so it cannot bypass that denial.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end exec of a real third-party binary through the approval pipeline (requires a trusted-dir executable); behavior is covered at the policy/validator layer instead.

## Test Plan

- [x] All tests in `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` pass: **98 passed** (90 existing + 8 new) under `vitest.infra.config.ts`
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:check` and `pnpm config:schema:check` pass (baseline in sync, no drift)
- [x] `oxlint` clean on changed files; no new type errors introduced
- [x] Real behavior proof captured above via the `openclaw` CLI and compiled validator
