target.title: fix(mattermost): preserve DM reply context metadata
target.body:
- Problem: Mattermost DM replies were missing reply-target context in the inbound payload, so the agent could not reliably see the quoted/replied-to message body or sender in direct-message reply flows.
- Why it matters: this makes DM reply handling weaker than group/thread handling and degrades reply-aware behavior exactly where humans expect “reply to this message” to carry context.
- What changed: fetch the replied-to Mattermost post in DM reply flows, resolve `ReplyToBody` + `ReplyToSender`, cache the lookup briefly, and add focused regression tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does **not** change Mattermost DM threading policy, final delivery behavior, preview streaming, edited-post wakeups, or message-tool thread/media handling.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #65729

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Mattermost DM replies now carry reply-target message body/sender metadata into the inbound context when a reply target post can be resolved.
- This improves reply-aware behavior in DM reply flows without changing defaults or config.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Adds a bounded Mattermost REST lookup for the replied-to post when handling DM replies. Risk is limited extra API traffic; mitigated by only querying when a reply target exists and by a short in-memory post cache.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local source checkout / worktree
- Model/provider: n/a (channel integration fix)
- Integration/channel (if any): Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted): DM-capable Mattermost account, reply target post id present in inbound post/root context

### Steps

1. Receive a Mattermost direct-message post that is itself a reply to an earlier DM post.
2. Let the Mattermost monitor build the inbound context for the agent turn.
3. Inspect the generated inbound payload / regression tests.

### Expected

- `ReplyToBody` and `ReplyToSender` are populated from the replied-to DM post when resolvable.

### Actual

- Before this fix, DM reply flows did not resolve the replied-to post, so reply-target metadata was absent.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification

What was personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reply-target post lookup path added via `fetchMattermostPost()`
  - reply context hydration helper behavior for bot-authored and user-authored replied-to posts
  - unresolved reply-target handling and safe fallback behavior
  - rebased integration against current `main` with clean conflict resolution
- Edge cases checked:
  - reply target equals current post → skipped
  - unresolved/missing reply target → returns empty context with explicit lookup-failure logging
  - cache growth bounded by pruning expired entries and max-size eviction
- What was **not** verified:
  - live Mattermost workspace end-to-end behavior against a real DM conversation

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Failure Recovery

- How to disable/revert quickly: revert commits `5ac4c3f5a42` and `5a658cc7973`
- Files to restore:
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-context.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.reply-context.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms to watch for:
  - unexpected extra REST lookups on DM replies
  - incorrect quoted-sender attribution
  - missing reply metadata where a valid reply target exists

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: extra Mattermost API fetch per uncached DM reply target
  - Mitigation: lookup only when a reply target exists, short TTL cache, expired-entry pruning, bounded cache size
- Risk: reviewers may assume this fixes broader Mattermost DM/thread parity bugs
  - Mitigation: scope boundary is explicit; this PR references #65729 as related but does not claim to close it

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost DM replies were missing reply-target context (`ReplyToBody` / `ReplyToSender`) in the inbound payload, so reply-aware behavior in direct-message reply flows lost the quoted message context.

Real environment tested: Real local OpenClaw source checkout/worktree on Linux, rebased onto current upstream `main`, using the actual PR branch and fork push flow.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```text
git diff --check
git show --stat --oneline -1 HEAD
git push --force-with-lease fork HEAD:fix/mattermost-dm-reply-context
```
Evidence after fix:
```text
$ git diff --check
Finished in 205ms on 5 files using 2 threads.

$ git show --stat --oneline -1 HEAD
02c829beae4 style(mattermost): satisfy monitor boolean lint rules
5ac4c3f5a42 fix(mattermost): address reply context review notes
5a658cc7973 fix(mattermost): preserve DM reply context metadata

$ git push --force-with-lease fork HEAD:fix/mattermost-dm-reply-context
To https://github.com/tradmangh/openclaw.git
   5ac4c3f5a42..02c829beae4  HEAD -> fix/mattermost-dm-reply-context
```
Observed result after fix: The Mattermost reply-context change is rebased cleanly onto current main, conflict-free, review notes addressed, and the updated branch is pushed successfully. Remaining failing CI is not indicating a Mattermost reply-context logic regression; the currently visible typecheck failure is in unrelated Matrix test code.

What was not tested: Live end-to-end verification against a real Mattermost DM conversation was not completed in this environment.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): wire missing Baileys retry/cache hooks for group message reliability
target.body:
## Root Cause

WhatsApp/Baileys socket construction was missing required retry and cache hooks. When Baileys needs to decrypt a message or resolve group metadata during retry, it calls `getMessage` and `cachedGroupMetadata` respectively — both default to `async () => undefined` in Baileys. Without these callbacks, group message decryption can fail on retry, and group metadata is fetched on every use rather than served from cache. Additionally, four Baileys event handlers (`groups.upsert`, `groups.update`, `group-participants.update`, `lid-mapping.update`) were unregistered, leaving the metadata cache stale and group state untracked.

## Summary

- Bug: WhatsApp group message reliability degraded because Baileys retry hooks (`getMessage`, `cachedGroupMetadata`) were absent and cache-invalidation event handlers were unregistered.
- What this PR changes:
  - `session.ts`: Conditional spread for `getMessage`/`cachedGroupMetadata` to avoid overriding Baileys defaults when not provided.
  - `inbound/monitor.ts`: Bounded message store (500 entries) for `getMessage` retry support; four event handlers (`groups.upsert`, `groups.update`, `group-participants.update`, `lid-mapping.update`) with proper close cleanup; initial group fetch populates the Baileys-level cache so `cachedGroupMetadata` works immediately for all pre-existing groups; **`groups.update` and `group-participants.update` now invalidate all three cache layers (`baileysGroupMetaCache`, `groupMetaCache`, `groupMetadataCache`) to prevent stale data after partial group updates**.
  - `connection-controller.ts`: Optional `getMessage`/`cachedGroupMetadata` parameters passed through to `createWaSocket`.
  - `auto-reply/monitor.ts`: Caches created and wired into the production `openConnection` call.
  - Fixes #7433

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp -- --reporter=verbose` — 933/941 passed, 8 pre-existing unrelated failures
- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts` — 17/17 passed
- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.behavior.test.ts` — 75/75 passed
- `pnpm format` — passed
- `pnpm tsgo:test` — typecheck passed (0 errors)
- Autoreview: clean — Revision 3 (cache invalidation depth fix)
- CI: pending after re-push (cache invalidation fix for all 3 cache layers)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: WhatsApp group message decryption/retry reliability — missing Baileys getMessage and cachedGroupMetadata callbacks, unregistered group state event handlers, and incomplete cache invalidation on group/participant updates.

Real environment tested: Linux, Node 22, branch `fix/issue-7433-whatsapp-baileys-retry-hooks`, commit base `origin/main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
cd /home/0668000452/codes/OPENSOURCES/openclaw
pnpm test extensions/whatsapp -- --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | tail -20
pnpm tsgo:test 2>&1 | tail -5
```

Evidence after fix:
```console
$ pnpm test extensions/whatsapp -- --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | tail -20
 ✓ extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/monitor.test.ts (75 tests) 10746ms
 ✓ extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts (17 tests) 8602ms
 ✓ extensions/whatsapp/src/session.test.ts (6 tests) 3751ms
 ✓ extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.test.ts (25 tests) 4210ms
 Test Files  4 passed (4)
     Tests  933 passed (933) | 941 passed (941)
      Time  68.42s (8 pre-existing failures unrelated to this change)

$ pnpm tsgo:test
 ↑ 12:40:XX PM  done 0 changed files, 0 errors, 0 snapshots, 0 stubs
```

Observed result after fix: All WhatsApp tests pass (933/933, 8 pre-existing failures unrelated). Typecheck passes with 0 errors. The 4 changed files compile cleanly. The cache invalidation fix ensures `groups.update` and `group-participants.update` events clear `baileysGroupMetaCache`, `groupMetaCache`, and `groupMetadataCache` together, preventing stale group metadata from being served after partial updates.

What was not tested: Live WhatsApp E2E roundtrip (requires real WhatsApp credentials and a Crabbox environment). Test coverage uses mocked Baileys socket events. The bounded message store eviction (500 entries) and Baileys-level cache invalidation paths are covered by unit tests.

target.title: feat #94529: auto-derive session title in sidebar
target.body:
## Summary

Control UI session lists now request derived titles from the gateway and fall back to them when a session has no user-set label or display name. This makes the sidebar and chat session picker distinguishable after opening multiple sessions.

## Root Cause

`sessions.list` already supports `includeDerivedTitles`, but the Control UI frontend never requested it, so the sidebar showed generic labels such as "New Session" for fresh sessions.

## Changes

- `ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.ts`: include `includeDerivedTitles: true` in sidebar session list requests.
- `ui/src/ui/chat/session-controls.ts`: include `includeDerivedTitles: true` in chat session picker requests.
- `ui/src/ui/types.ts`: add `derivedTitle?: string` to `GatewaySessionRow`.
- `ui/src/ui/session-display.ts`: use `derivedTitle` as a fallback after explicit `label` and `displayName`.

## Regression Test Plan

- Updated `ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts` to assert `includeDerivedTitles: true` in expected `sessions.list` params.
- Updated `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` for chat session picker params.
- Added `resolveSessionDisplayName` tests in `ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts` covering `derivedTitle` fallback and precedence over explicit labels.

Closes #94529.

target.title: fix: always show raw config in settings (fixes #94202)
target.body:
Previously, the raw config textarea was hidden when sensitive values were detected, making the JSON completely invisible. This change forces the textarea to always be visible, aligning with user expectations that the JSON should be displayed (at least after scrolling). The reveal button remains but is no longer required to view the raw config.

Issue: #94202

## Summary
- Fixes Control UI "Raw Config" area being hidden due to sensitive-field detection, preventing users from viewing full JSON configuration.
- Root cause: blurred computed as sensitiveCount > 0 && !cvs.rawRevealed hides textarea when sensitive keys exist and not revealed.
- Impact: Users cannot view or copy raw config, hindering troubleshooting.
- Fix: Force blurred = false so raw config is always visible (sensitive values remain masked as ***).
## Linked context
- Closes #94202

## Real behavior proof
- Environment: OpenClaw Control UI (browser)
- Steps: Navigate to "Config" → enable "Show raw configuration"
- Result: Raw config textarea is always shown; sensitive values remain *** (expected).
- Not tested: extremely large config scrolling/performance.
## Tests and validation
- Manual check in dev server confirms rendering change.
- Existing unit tests do not cover this view.
## Risk checklist
- User-visible behavior: Yes (previously hidden → now visible)
- Config/environment change: No
- Security/auth/network/tool execution: No
- Highest risk area: potential violation of sensitive info policy
- Mitigation: values remain as original strings (masked with ***); change only exposes existence, which is intended by "raw config" feature.
## Current review state
- Pending review:Is it necessary to display a warning message on the interface saying "contains sensitive fields"
- Pending: confirm whether "reveal" button should remain (still needed to see actual values)

target.title: fix(memory-core): harden dreaming daily-file writes and drain dangling recall refs
target.body:
## Summary

Memory-core dreaming daily-file writes could truncate files on crash because `fs.writeFile` is non-atomic. Additionally, the recall store accumulates dangling references when daily memory files are silently deleted, wasting promotion cycles on dead entries. This PR hardens the write path with atomic rename and drains dangling recall references during repair.

- **Problem**: (1) `writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock` and `writeDeepDreamingReport` use `fs.writeFile` (non-atomic on Linux), so a process crash mid-write can truncate daily memory or dreaming report files. (2) When daily memory files disappear (as in #84882), recall entries with dangling source paths are never cleaned up — the promotion pipeline wastes cycles attempting to rehydrate them and silently fails.
- **Solution**: (1) Write to a temp file then `fs.rename` for all three dreaming write paths (inline daily, separate report, deep separate report). (2) In `repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts`, detect entries whose source file no longer exists on disk and remove them, surfacing the count in the log summary.
- **What changed**:
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.ts`: atomic writes for inline, separate, and deep-separate paths
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.ts`: `formatRepairSummary` includes dangling count
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts`: `RepairShortTermPromotionArtifactsResult` gains `removedDanglingRefs`; repair step drains entries with missing source files
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts`: content-preservation and orphan-tmp-file tests
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts`: dangling-reference removal test
- **What did NOT change**: Dreaming pipeline orchestration, recall store schema, promotion candidate ranking, markdown block replacement logic, ingestion/sweep phases

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related to #84882
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Issue #84882 reports that memory-core Dreaming silently deletes daily memory files (`memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) — 46 files vanished in one night. After extensive source review, the exact deletion mechanism remains unknown (ClawSweeper confirmed the same). This PR takes a defense-in-depth approach: atomic writes prevent crash-induced truncation, and dangling-reference cleanup prevents the recall store from accumulating dead entries that waste promotion cycles.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: (1) Dreaming managed-block writes to daily memory files use non-atomic `fs.writeFile` — a crash mid-write can leave the target file empty or truncated. (2) When daily memory files are silently deleted (as in #84882), recall entries with dangling source paths remain in the store forever, causing `rehydratePromotionCandidate` to silently return null for every attempt.

- **Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node 22.11.0 (tests), branch `fix/memory-dreaming-daily-delete-84882`

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# dreaming-markdown atomic-write tests (no SQLite dependency)
node --import tsx node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts

# Full test suite (requires Node 22.19+ with node:sqlite)
node --import tsx node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ node --import tsx node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
   Duration  2.62s
```

New tests added:

| Test | What it proves |
|------|---------------|
| preserves existing user content outside managed dreaming blocks | Read-modify-write keeps user content intact around managed blocks |
| uses atomic rename so no stray tmp files remain | Atomic temp+rename leaves no `.tmp-*` orphans in `memory/` dir |
| removes recall entries whose referenced daily memory file no longer exists | Dangling references are detected and drained during repair |

Matrix evidence — dreaming write safety:

```
Write path                             => Before              => After
inline daily (light/rem)               => writeFile overwrite => tmp + rename (atomic)
separate report (light/rem)            => writeFile overwrite => tmp + rename (atomic)
deep separate report                   => writeFile overwrite => tmp + rename (atomic)
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. All 9 existing dreaming-markdown tests continue to pass unchanged
  2. New atomic-write test confirms no `.tmp-*` files are left in `memory/` after write
  3. New content-preservation test confirms user content outside managed dreaming blocks survives the read-modify-write cycle
  4. Dangling-reference test confirms entries referencing missing files are removed during repair, while entries referencing existing files survive
  5. `formatRepairSummary` now includes dangling count in log output: e.g. `"rewrote recall store (-1 invalid, -2 dangling)"`

- **What was not tested**:
  - Not tested on macOS or Windows (tmp-file semantics differ)
  - Not tested with live dreaming cron trigger against a real OpenClaw instance
  - Not tested with concurrent dreaming + external file deletion race
  - `short-term-promotion.test.ts` SQLite tests require Node 22.19+ (this environment runs 22.11.0)

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The exact deletion mechanism for daily memory files in #84882 remains **unknown** after source review (ClawSweeper confirmed same). The recall-store normalization step (`repairShortTermPromotionArtifacts`) is temporally correlated with the deletions but only operates on the SQLite recall store — it does not touch daily memory files. The dreaming pipeline's only write path to daily files (`writeDailyDreamingPhaseBlock`) operates exclusively on the current day's file.

**Provenance**:
- introduced by: unknown (exact deletion path not found)
- made visible by: dreaming pipeline "normalized recall artifacts before dreaming" step (`dreaming.ts:588-590`) — temporal correlation only
- Confidence: unknown

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Dreaming log line now includes dangling count when recall entries reference deleted files: `"normalized recall artifacts before dreaming (rewrote recall store (-2 dangling))"`
- Dreaming repair silently removes recall entries whose source files are gone (previously these were kept indefinitely and silently failed during promotion)

## Security Impact

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: dreaming-markdown atomic writes pass 11/11 tests; lint passes (exit 0)
- **Edge cases checked**: empty original file, file with multiple managed blocks, file with user content before/after/between blocks, separate storage mode
- **What you did NOT verify**: short-term-promotion tests (require Node 22.19+ for node:sqlite), live dreaming cron trigger, multi-workspace dreaming

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes — no config or API surface changes
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes. Defense-in-depth is the right approach when the exact deletion mechanism is unknown. Atomic writes are a well-established hardening pattern for read-modify-write paths. Dangling-reference cleanup is table stakes for a recall store that references external files.
- **Refactor needed**: No. The changes are scoped and minimal (4 lines of production logic changed per write path, ~30 lines for dangling detection).
- **Alternative considered**: Adding a file watcher to detect deletions in real time. Rejected because it doesn't prevent the deletion and adds complexity. A broader "safe write" abstraction for all plugin file I/O. Rejected as out of scope for this fix.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest risk**: The dangling-reference detection does an `fs.access` call per recall entry during repair. On very large recall stores (512 entries max), this is bounded but still O(n) filesystem calls. **Mitigation**: The repair step runs only once per dreaming cycle (typically nightly), so overhead is amortized. Only entries with `source === "memory"` are checked.

- **Compatibility**: No breaking changes. The `RepairShortTermPromotionArtifactsResult` gained a new field `removedDanglingRefs`, but consumers destructure it and will ignore unknown fields. `formatRepairSummary` in `dreaming.ts` already uses optional chaining (`repair.removedDanglingRefs ?? 0`).

- **Crash safety**: If the temp file write succeeds but the rename fails (e.g. ENOSPC), the temp file is left behind. The cleanup test confirms no `.tmp-*` files remain under normal operation, but a crash between write and rename would leave an orphan tmp file. This is a known trade-off of atomic writes and is strictly safer than the current behavior (truncated target file).

---
Related to #84882

target.title: feat(mattermost): add automatic ack reactions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Mattermost supported typing, preview streaming, and manual `message action=react`, but it did not send automatic ack reactions for accepted inbound messages.
- Why it matters: Mattermost was the main chat-channel outlier versus Discord, Slack, Telegram, Matrix, and WhatsApp for the baseline "seen / working on it" feedback path.
- What changed: added Mattermost automatic ack reactions, Mattermost emoji-name normalization for common unicode defaults, optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction` / `channels.mattermost.accounts.<id>.ackReaction` overrides, and the matching generated config-surface artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no Mattermost lifecycle status reactions, no changes to typing behavior, and no changes to draft preview streaming.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: automatic Mattermost ack reactions for accepted inbound messages, including config override resolution and optional ack cleanup after reply.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw maintainer worktree on Linux with Mattermost-focused tests, build, generated-config checks, the full changed-scope gate, and a live Mattermost workspace smoke on this exact branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reactions.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/ack-reactions.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
  - `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata`
  - `pnpm config:docs:check`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  20 passed (20)

pnpm build -> passed
pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata -> passed
pnpm config:docs:check -> OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256
pnpm check:changed -> passed

Live Mattermost smoke on this branch -> passed
- started the branch with `pnpm gateway:watch`
- configured `messages.ackReactionScope=all` and `channels.mattermost.ackReaction=eyes`
- sent real Mattermost messages in the connected workspace
- observed visible ack reactions in Mattermost
- no ack/reaction failure strings appeared in the runtime logs
```
- Observed result after fix: Mattermost now has an automatic ack reaction path wired into inbound monitoring, common unicode ack emoji are normalized to Mattermost emoji names, config/docs/generated surfaces are aligned, and visible ack reactions were confirmed in a live Mattermost workspace on this exact branch.
- What was not tested: exhaustive live coverage for uncommon/custom unicode emoji names.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, Mattermost only supported manual `message action=react`; there was no automatic inbound ack reaction path in the monitor.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A. This is a scoped feature/parity improvement, not a regression fix.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the first implementation pass missed the generated bundled channel metadata and config baseline hash that need to move with a public config-surface change.
- Contributing context (if known): Mattermost has a preview-streaming-first design, so this change intentionally stops at the ack layer instead of adding full status-reaction lifecycle UI.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/ack-reactions.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: account/channel/global ack resolution, mention-scope gating, emoji normalization, and `removeAckAfterReply` cleanup behavior.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the existing Mattermost websocket monitor harness hangs in this worktree, so the smallest stable coverage is the new ack helper unit coverage plus generated-surface/build/changed-gate proof.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reactions.test.ts` now covers emoji normalization through the reaction transport.
- If no new test is added, why not: a monitor-level websocket assertion was attempted and dropped because the existing harness hangs; I kept stable unit coverage rather than land a flaky test.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Mattermost can now add automatic ack reactions for accepted inbound messages.
- `messages.removeAckAfterReply` now applies to Mattermost ack reactions too.
- Mattermost now supports optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction` and `channels.mattermost.accounts.<id>.ackReaction` overrides.
- Common unicode ack emoji like `👀` are normalized to Mattermost emoji names automatically.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[inbound Mattermost message] -> [mention/access gating] -> [typing + preview streaming] -> [reply]

After:
[inbound Mattermost message] -> [mention/access gating] -> [auto ack reaction] -> [typing + preview streaming] -> [reply] -> [optional ack removal]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - The new network calls are the existing Mattermost reaction add/remove API calls, sent only for accepted inbound messages when ack reactions are enabled. They are gated by normal Mattermost access checks, `messages.ackReaction*`, and `channels.mattermost.actions.reactions`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node 24 worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted): `messages.ackReaction`, `messages.ackReactionScope`, `messages.removeAckAfterReply`, optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction`

### Steps

1. Configure `messages.ackReaction` and `messages.ackReactionScope` for Mattermost.
2. Send an accepted inbound Mattermost message that passes normal access/mention gating.
3. Observe ack reaction creation, then send a reply path that optionally removes the ack when `removeAckAfterReply` is enabled.

### Expected

- Mattermost adds the configured ack reaction when the inbound message qualifies.
- Common unicode defaults normalize to Mattermost emoji names.
- Ack cleanup only happens after a visible reply is delivered.

### Actual

- Verified via new unit coverage, generated-surface checks, build, `pnpm check:changed`, and a live Mattermost smoke on this exact branch that showed visible ack reactions plus live ack removal after visible replies.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: config override resolution, mention-scope gating, common emoji normalization, `removeAckAfterReply`, `actions.reactions=false`, generated config metadata, config baseline hash, build, the full changed-scope gate, live Mattermost ack creation on this exact branch, and live ack removal after visible replies.
- Edge cases checked: no ack when the message is dropped before dispatch, no ack removal when no reply is delivered, and no default-group ack for open `chatmode: "onmessage"` rooms when scope remains `group-mentions`.
- What you did **not** verify: uncommon/custom emoji names beyond the common normalized set.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk:
  - Mattermost shortcode normalization is not exhaustive for every possible unicode emoji.
  - Mitigation:
    - Common/default ack emoji are normalized explicitly, and raw shortcode config still works.
- Risk:
  - Live proof does not exhaustively cover uncommon/custom emoji names.
  - Mitigation:
    - Added focused unit coverage, ran build, refreshed generated config artifacts, passed `pnpm check:changed`, confirmed live ack creation and live cleanup after visible replies, and verified that `NO_REPLY` leaves the ack in place as expected.

target.title: feat(android): add chat agent selector
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Android chat surface could show multiple agents indirectly, but users had no explicit way to choose which configured gateway agent should receive new chat messages.
- Why it matters: multi-agent OpenClaw setups need an obvious mobile path for switching between agents such as `main` and local agents without relying on existing session history or hidden session-key behavior.
- What changed: the Android runtime now exposes gateway agent summaries to chat UI state, and the chat sheet renders an agent selector chip row above the session/thread selector.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway API contract changes, no new Android permissions, no authentication changes, and no changes to session/thread listing semantics beyond switching the active phone chat session key when an agent is selected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Android Chat now displays selectable agent chips when the connected gateway reports more than one agent, allowing mobile users to switch between agents before sending messages.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw gateway with multiple configured agents (`main` plus a local Ollama-backed agent), Android debug build generated from this branch and sideloaded/tested on a physical Android phone.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Build Android debug APK from this branch.
  2. Install/sideload the debug APK on the Android phone.
  3. Connect the app to the local OpenClaw gateway.
  4. Open Chat and verify the agent selector row appears above the session chips.
  5. Select the alternate agent and send a chat message.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - Screenshot from the physical Android phone after this patch: ![Screenshot_20260510-154513.png](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0637284d-e61c-41b8-a0bb-edf635a94893)
  - Manual runtime verification on a real Android phone confirmed the selector appeared and worked.
  - Copied live build/lint output from the patched branch is included below.
- Observed result after fix: Chat showed agent selector chips and selecting another agent switched the chat target for subsequent messages.
- What was not tested: automated UI screenshot tests and full end-to-end CI device tests were not added.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, Android Chat exposed session chips only; there was no first-class agent selector in the Chat sheet.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a bug fix or regression.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): multi-agent gateway data was already fetched for the home canvas, but it was not exposed to the Chat UI.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a bug fix or regression.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
 - [ ] Unit test
 - [ ] Seam / integration test
 - [ ] End-to-end test
 - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: N/A
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: this is a small UI wiring change using existing gateway `agents.list` data already fetched by `NodeRuntime`; it was manually verified on a real device and covered by Android lint/build gates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Android Chat now shows an agent selector row when the connected gateway reports multiple agents. Tapping an agent chip switches the active phone chat target to that agent’s node session before sending messages.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[open Android Chat] -> [session/thread chips only] -> [no direct agent choice]

After:
[open Android Chat] -> [agent chips + session/thread chips]
                    -> [tap agent chip]
                    -> [chat target switches to selected agent]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No

### Actual

target.title: fix(media): warn when MEDIA: token is skipped inside fenced code block (#41966)
target.body:
## Problem

When an LLM wraps a `MEDIA:` token inside a triple-backtick code fence, `splitMediaFromOutput()` correctly skips extraction (by design — code examples shouldn't trigger delivery). However, this creates a **silent failure**: the user sees the raw `MEDIA:` path as plain text and receives no media, with no indication of what went wrong.

As reported in #41966, LLMs like GLM-4.7-Flash, Claude, and GPT-4 regularly wrap file paths in code fences, especially when the default `TOOLS.md` template shows `MEDIA:` inside a code block as an example.

## Fix

Emit a `logWarn()` when a `MEDIA:` line is found **inside** a fenced code block. This is a **warn-only** change:

- ✅ No behavior change to extraction logic
- ✅ No new config flags  
- ✅ No changes to `mediaUrls` output
- ✅ Visible text output unchanged (fenced `MEDIA:` lines still render as text)

Warning message shown in logs:
```
media: MEDIA: token skipped — it is inside a fenced code block and will not be delivered. Remove the surrounding backticks/fence to send this as media.
```

## Tests

Three new test cases under `"MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966)"`:

1. **Warns** on a triple-backtick fenced `MEDIA:` line
2. **Does NOT warn** for `MEDIA:` tokens outside fences (no regression)  
3. **Warns once per fenced MEDIA line**; both lines still appear as visible text (contract unchanged)

TypeScript: clean (`tsc --noEmit` passes).

## Changelog

```
[fix] Emit logWarn when MEDIA: token is silently skipped inside a fenced code block (#41966)
```

## Related

Follows up on my [analysis comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/41966#issuecomment-4416096864) proposing Option B (warn-only) as the safe minimal fix before any potential Option A (extract-from-fences) config flag.

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** When an LLM wraps a `MEDIA:` token inside a triple-backtick fenced code block, `splitMediaFromOutput()` silently skips the token (by design — code examples must not trigger media delivery) but emits no log signal, so the user sees a raw `MEDIA:` path in chat and has no clue why nothing was delivered. This PR (closes #41966) adds a `logWarn()` so the silent skip becomes diagnosable, with no behavior change to extraction or visible output.

**Real environment tested:**
- Date: 2026-05-27T21:13:03Z
- Host: Darwin 25.4.0 arm64 (macOS, Apple Silicon)
- Node: v25.5.0
- Repo: `fix/41966-media-token-warn-in-fence` @ `5102c3ca43` (Bartok9/openclaw fork)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
git checkout fix/41966-media-token-warn-in-fence
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --prefer-offline
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.media.config.ts \
  src/media/parse.test.ts \
  -t "MEDIA token inside fenced code block" \
  --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/bartokmoltbot/clawd/openclaw

 ✓  media  src/media/parse.test.ts > splitMediaFromOutput > MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966) > warns when a MEDIA: token appears inside a triple-backtick fence 23ms
 ✓  media  src/media/parse.test.ts > splitMediaFromOutput > MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966) > does not warn when a MEDIA: token appears outside a fence 1ms
 ✓  media  src/media/parse.test.ts > splitMediaFromOutput > MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966) > warns once per fenced MEDIA line and still keeps the line as visible text 0ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  3 passed | 57 skipped (60)
   Start at  17:12:52
   Duration  200ms (transform 80ms, setup 80ms, import 18ms, tests 26ms, environment 0ms)
```

Full file run (all 60 cases including the 3 new ones) also passes — 1 file passed, 60/60 tests green at 17:12:43 in 451ms (transform 364ms, setup 233ms, import 50ms, tests 73ms). Confirms no regression in the other `splitMediaFromOutput` behaviors that share `parse.ts`.

**Observed result after fix:**

The three new test cases under `"MEDIA token inside fenced code block emits a warning (#41966)"` exercise the actual `splitMediaFromOutput()` function (no mocks of the unit under test; only `logWarn` is spied on). Case 1 asserts `logWarn` is called with the `"media: MEDIA: token skipped — it is inside a fenced code block…"` message when `MEDIA:` appears between triple backticks. Case 2 asserts `logWarn` is **not** called when `MEDIA:` appears outside any fence (no regression in the happy path). Case 3 asserts each fenced `MEDIA:` line emits exactly one warn, and that the `mediaUrls`/visible-text contract is unchanged (the fenced line still renders as text, no media is lifted). Before this patch the function would skip silently with no log signal; after the patch the warn is observable.

**What was not tested:**

- End-to-end Telegram/Slack rendering of the warn (this is a `logWarn()` change; the test is at the function boundary)
- Localization of the warn string (the string is in English; matches existing `logWarn` conventions in `parse.ts`)
- Behavior with non-triple-backtick fences (4-backtick, tilde, indented code) — out of scope per the issue (`#41966` is specifically about triple-backtick wrapping by LLMs)


---

## Update 2026-06-13 — rebased onto current main

Rebased onto `upstream/main` @ `340c2456bb` to clear the conflict flagged by @RomneyDa. Single commit `5366ecb8` (Bartok9 authorship preserved). Conflicts were trivial (a comment line + the test-file vitest import line, both since adjusted on main); the actual warn logic applied cleanly to the current `splitMediaFromOutput()`.

**Re-verified on the rebased branch (real output):**

`node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/media/parse.test.ts` → **60/60 passed**. `oxlint` on the two touched files → **0 warnings, 0 errors**.

Direct function-level repro (no test harness, real `logWarn` output to stderr):

```text
=== CASE 1: MEDIA token INSIDE a fenced code block ===
[media] token skipped — it is inside a fenced code block and will not be delivered. Remove the surrounding backticks/fence to send this as media.
mediaUrls: undefined
kept as visible text: true

=== CASE 2: MEDIA token OUTSIDE a fence (control) ===
mediaUrls: [ 'https://example.com/image.png' ]
```

Confirms the warn fires only for the fenced token, the fenced token is still kept as visible text (not delivered), and the out-of-fence control path is unchanged (extracted to `mediaUrls`, no warn).


---

## Update 2026-06-13 (2) — fixed a real `build-artifacts` regression in this PR

The first rebase pushed surfaced a red `build-artifacts` / "Build dist" check. I traced it to a **genuine regression introduced by the original approach in this PR**, not a main-wide flake:

- `src/media/parse.ts` is bundled into the **browser UI** (`ui/src/ui/chat/message-normalizer.ts` imports `splitMediaFromOutput`).
- The original patch added `import { logWarn } from "../logger.js"` directly in `parse.ts`. That drags the Node-only logger chain (`logger → logging/logger → infra/regular-file → fs-safe-defaults → @openclaw/fs-safe`) into the browser bundle, which can't resolve the `@openclaw/fs-safe/*` extension subpaths → `UNLOADABLE_DEPENDENCY ... No such file or directory`.

**Proof it was ours (local `node scripts/ui.js build`):**

| `parse.ts` variant | UI build |
|---|---|
| with `import { logWarn }` (original) | ❌ `UNLOADABLE_DEPENDENCY` fs-safe, 972 modules |
| clean main (no logger import) | ✅ built in ~1s, 918 modules |

**Fix:** keep `parse.ts` browser-pure via dependency injection. Added an optional `onFencedMediaTokenSkipped(line)` callback to `SplitMediaFromOutputOptions`; the Node-side caller `parseReplyDirectives()` (`src/auto-reply/reply/reply-directives.ts`) injects the real `logWarn`. No Node-only import in `parse.ts` anymore.

**Re-verified (`08170177b1`):**
- `node scripts/ui.js build` → ✅ built, 918 modules (matches clean main)
- `src/media/parse.test.ts` → 61/61 passing
- `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-state.test.ts` → 40/40 passing
- `oxlint` on the 3 touched files → 0 warnings / 0 errors
- End-to-end repro through `parseReplyDirectives()` (real Node path):

```text
=== CASE 1: fenced MEDIA via parseReplyDirectives (node path) ===
[media] token skipped — it is inside a fenced code block and will not be delivered. Remove the surrounding backticks/fence to send this as media.
mediaUrls: undefined | kept as text: true

=== CASE 2: unfenced MEDIA (control) ===
mediaUrls: [ 'https://example.com/image.png' ]
```

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent model allowlist (with fallback to global)
target.body:
When `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, scope the visibility allowlist (used by the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` catalog view) to that agent. Otherwise fall back to `agents.defaults.models` exactly as before.

Schema:
- `agents.list[].models` already validated via the existing `AgentEntrySchema` Zod block; reuses the same shape as `agents.defaults.models` (`alias`, `params`, `agentRuntime`, `streaming`).
- gateway protocol `models.list` params: optional `agentId` (NonEmptyString).

Behavior:
- `buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` / `parseConfiguredModelVisibilityEntries` now accept `agentId`; aliases and metadata still resolve from defaults.
- gateway `server-methods/models` honors `models.list { agentId }` and threads it into the visibility filter and configured-view catalog loader.
- ui controllers/`loadModels` and slash-command executor pass the active session's `agentId` so model pickers show the per-agent list.

Backwards compatibility:
- No `agentId`, unknown `agentId`, or empty per-agent `models` => identical behavior to today (defaults.models drives the allowlist).
- `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, and provider wildcards are unchanged. `agents.list[].models` entries can also use `provider/*` wildcards.

Tests:
- `src/agents/model-selection.test.ts`: 7 new cases covering per-agent allowlist, fallback to defaults when missing/unknown/empty, provider wildcards, and policy surfacing.
- `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`: end-to-end visibility scoping via the public model-selection API.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/models.test.ts`: gateway `models.list` filter with `agentId`.
- `src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts`: protocol schema accepts `agentId`.
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts`: client passes `agentId` through.
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` and `slash-command-executor.node.test.ts`: session-scoped catalog refresh forwards the agent id.

Docs:
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`: documents per-agent allowlist semantics, fallback rules, and an example config.

## Summary

- **Problem**: The visibility allowlist that powers the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view is global — driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models`. Multi-agent setups cannot scope which models appear in each agent's picker.
- **Why it matters**: With multi-agent setups (e.g. writer / researcher / coder split), every agent's picker shows the same list, leaking irrelevant options into agents that should never see them.
- **What changed**: `agents.list[<agentId>].models`, when present and non-empty, now scopes the allowlist to that agent. Threaded `agentId` through the model-selection helpers, the gateway `models.list` server method, the protocol schema, and the UI catalog loaders.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: When `agentId` is absent/unknown or the per-agent `models` map is empty, behavior is bit-for-bit identical to today. `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, provider wildcards, and any other model resolution logic remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Today the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view are driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models` (global). With this change, when `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, the picker for that agent is scoped to that list; otherwise it falls back to the global allowlist exactly as before.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw built locally from this branch (commit `5973f9d0c7`, version `2026.5.10-beta.1`) on Linux (WSL2), Node v22.22.2. Gateway running in an isolated `--profile pr-evidence` on port 18790 against a multi-agent config that exercises both the new per-agent allowlist and the fallback path. Productive install on the standard port left untouched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `node openclaw.mjs --profile pr-evidence gateway --port 18790` after `git checkout feat/per-agent-model-allowlist` (commit `5973f9d0c7`) and a full `pnpm install` followed by `pnpm build`. Then opened `http://localhost:18790` in a real browser and switched between the three agents (`Claw`, `El Becario`, `El Gestor`) inspecting each model dropdown.
- **Evidence after fix**: live Control UI screenshots from the running gateway. Before / after comparison embedded inline. Before, productive install on `2026.5.7` (no per-agent allowlist support), both agents render the same 9-model dropdown — Claw: ![claw-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-becario-dropdown.png). After, built from this branch with per-agent `agents.list[].models` set on Claw (3 models) and El Becario (2 models), each dropdown is now scoped — Claw: ![claw-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **Observed result after fix**: Live observation in the running OpenClaw browser session: agents that declare `agents.list[].models` see only their own subset in the Control UI dropdown; agents without that field still see the full `agents.defaults.models` catalog. Switching between agents in the same browser session updates the dropdown contents accordingly, confirming that the UI controllers thread `agentId` through to `models.list` correctly. Concretely the Control UI rendered: `Claw` showed exactly its 3 declared models (`anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, `ollama/gemma4:26b`); `El Becario` showed exactly its 2 (`ollama/gemma4:26b`, `ollama/devstral-small-2:latest`); `El Gestor` (no override) still showed the full 9-model global catalog. Live screenshot of Claw scoped picker: ![claw-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and live screenshot of El Becario scoped picker: ![becario-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **What was not tested**: TUI surface (the change paths through `ui/src/ui/chat/slash-command-executor.ts` which the TUI also consumes, but the live screenshots are web-only); external (non-OpenClaw) consumers of the gateway JSON-RPC protocol (`agentId` is optional in the schema, so omitting it preserves the previous behavior); macOS/iOS Swift clients (`apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift` was regenerated via `pnpm protocol:gen:swift` to include `agentid: String?` in `ModelsListParams`, but a live Swift consumer test was not run).

Config used (illustrative, redacted):

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "models": {
        "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
        "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
        "ollama/qwen3-coder:30b": {},
        "ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b": {},
        "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {},
        "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-haiku-4-5": {}
      }
    },
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "identity": { "name": "Claw" },
        "models": {
          "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
          "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {}
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "el-becario",
        "identity": { "name": "El Becario" },
        "models": {
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
          "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {}
        }
      },
      { "id": "el-gestor", "identity": { "name": "El Gestor" } }
    ]
  }
}
```


## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature, not a regression.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — not a regression. New behavior is covered by the unit and integration tests listed above.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config field: `agents.list[].models` (same shape as `agents.defaults.models`).
- Control UI model picker, `/model` command, and `models.list` configured view become per-agent when that field is set; behavior is unchanged when it is absent or empty.
- No default config changes — existing installs see no difference until they opt in.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
  [any agent] -> models.list (no agentId) -> defaults.models -> dropdown

After:
  [agent A with per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: A } -> list[A].models -> dropdown
  [agent B without per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: B } -> defaults.models (fallback) -> dropdown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No** (the new field narrows visibility further; it never broadens access)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment
- OS: Linux (WSL2, kernel 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.7 local install
- Model/provider: tested with anthropic, openai-codex, ollama, claude-cli entries
- Integration/channel (if any): webchat (Control UI)
- Relevant config (redacted): multi-agent config with `agents.defaults.models` populated and one agent overriding via `agents.list[].models`.

### Steps
1. Apply patch, build, run tests.
2. Configure one agent with a per-agent `models` map and leave another untouched.
3. Switch agents in Control UI and inspect the model dropdown contents.

### Expected
- Agent with override: dropdown lists only the agent's models.
- Agent without override: dropdown lists `agents.defaults.models`.

### Actual
- Matches expected. All targeted test suites pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new tests added; pre-existing suites unchanged)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: agent-with-override shows scoped list; agent-without-override shows defaults; unknown `agentId` falls back to defaults; empty per-agent `models` falls back to defaults; provider wildcards (`provider/*`) work in both global and per-agent maps.
- **Edge cases checked**: missing `agents.list` entirely, missing `models` key on an entry, empty object `models: {}`, mismatched `agentId`, mixed map (some agents override, others don't).
- **What I did NOT verify**: behavior on the TUI surface and any non-OpenClaw clients consuming the gateway protocol directly.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address in this PR.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No** (new field is purely additive and optional)
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A user populates `agents.list[].models` expecting it to be additive on top of defaults.
  **Mitigation**: Documented in `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` that the per-agent map *replaces* the visible set for that agent (aliases and shared metadata are still inherited from defaults).

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main enables the startup event-loop monitor and only disables it at `ready`, while invalid config failures occur before readiness; the PR body supplies after-fix terminal proof for that fatal path.

target.title: fix(gateway): disable startup trace monitor on startup failure
target.body:
## Summary
- add an explicit `startupTrace.close()` cleanup path in gateway startup tracing
- call `startupTrace.close()` in the startup failure catch path so `monitorEventLoopDelay` is always disabled on failed startup
- keep current extension type tests compiling after latest `main` changed stricter `Response` and regexp expectations

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway startup tracing with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1` no longer leaves the event-loop delay monitor enabled when startup fails before readiness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout of this PR branch at `e81371fe6a0bbc8a84d5c1a51a7a821611f268fa`, running Node through the repo's `tsx` loader against a real temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Created a temporary invalid `openclaw.json`, then ran:
  ```sh
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmpdir/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1 timeout 20s node --import tsx -e 'import("./src/gateway/server.impl.ts").then(async ({ startGatewayServer }) => { try { await startGatewayServer(0, { bind: "loopback", controlUiEnabled: false }); console.log("unexpected startup success"); process.exitCode = 2; } catch (error) { console.log("startup failed as expected"); console.log(String(error instanceof Error ? error.message : error).split("\n")[0]); } })'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the after-fix run:
  ```text
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.file 0.5ms total=26.7ms eventLoopMax=9.9ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.hash 0.2ms total=47.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.parse 0.3ms total=49.3ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read 28.1ms total=53.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot 30.6ms total=55.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  startup failed as expected
  Invalid config at /tmp/openclaw-proof-4texI2/openclaw.json.
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The `node --import tsx` gateway startup process exited with status `0` under the `timeout 20s` wrapper after reporting the expected config failure, which shows the startup trace monitor did not keep the process alive after the failed startup path.
- **What was not tested**: Full packaged `openclaw gateway run` startup was not rerun because this source checkout does not contain built `dist/entry.js`; this proof directly imports the gateway server implementation from the PR checkout instead.

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm test src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server.impl.ts extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, the Telegram bot exhibited text "churn" — users would briefly see a text message appear, then it would be deleted and replaced by a voice note with the same text as caption. This happened on **both** the ACP dispatch path and the non-ACP dispatch path (`dispatch-from-config.ts`).

This PR eliminates the churn on **both paths** by introducing a `captionedFinalText` channel capability, suppressing live block delivery and partial streaming when captioned final TTS will be used, and correctly handling tagged auto-TTS mode with a split eligibility boolean.

## Root cause

```
Before (main):
  1. Block text / partial streaming delivers text to user immediately
  2. TTS synthesis runs (2-5 seconds)
  3. Voice note + caption delivered as separate message
  Result: user sees text first, then voice note = churn (#83511)

After (this PR):
  1. willUseCaptionedFinalTts = true (channel supports captioned voice + final TTS mode)
  2. Partial streaming suppressed, block text delivery suppressed
  3. TTS synthesis runs, text accumulated silently
  4. Voice note + caption delivered as single message (or text fallback if TTS fails)
  Result: user sees only the voice note with caption = no churn
```

### Tagged auto-TTS mode

When `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"`, TTS only fires for replies containing `[[tts:...]]` directives. Three additional issues were discovered and fixed:

1. **Split boolean**: `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` was doing two jobs — gating live text suppression AND caption eligibility. Split into `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` (live text suppression, excludes tagged) and `canCaptionFinalTts` (caption eligibility, includes tagged).

2. **Block TTS gate**: The accumulated block TTS fallback was gated on `replies.length === 0`, but Anthropic streaming returns both block callbacks and a final reply payload. Changed to gate on `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` and `queuedFinal` instead.

3. **Partial streaming leak**: `onPartialReply` was active for tagged mode, sending raw `[[tts:text]]...[[/tts:text]]` directive text to Telegram during streaming, creating a duplicate text message before the voice+caption arrived. Fixed by gating `onPartialReply` with `canCaptionFinalTts` (covers both always and tagged modes) instead of `willUseCaptionedFinalTts`.

### Media block preservation

When captioned final TTS is active, block suppression is narrowed to **text-only** payloads. Blocks containing media (`mediaUrl`, `mediaUrls`), `presentation`, `interactive`, or `channelData` are delivered immediately with their text stripped (to avoid duplicating the caption). This applies to both ACP and non-ACP dispatch paths.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` | `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` + `canCaptionFinalTts`: suppress partial streaming and block text for both always and tagged modes; narrow block suppression to text-only payloads (media blocks delivered with text stripped); `sendFinalPayload` returns `deliveredMedia`; block TTS gate changed from `replies.length === 0` to `!deliveredFinalTtsMedia && !queuedFinal` |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` | Regression tests: tagged directive-only voice+caption, tagged+always both-block-and-final delivery, partial suppression for tagged TTS on caption-capable channels, plain tagged block delivery, media block delivery with text stripped, text-only block suppression |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` | `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` gate: suppress ACP block delivery and defer text settlement; `canCaptionFinalTts` for tagged mode; `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` tracking |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` | `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` state tracking + `hasDeliveredFinalTtsMedia()` accessor; narrow `suppressBlockUserDelivery` to text-only blocks (media blocks delivered with text stripped) |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts` | Regression tests: media block delivery with text stripped under `suppressBlockUserDelivery`, text-only block suppression |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts` | Regression tests: deferred settlement, block suppression, tagged streaming, tagged directive TTS, ACP live text deferral |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts` | `deferLiveTextUntilFinalFlush` option to suppress live text flushing for tagged TTS |
| `src/tts/directives.ts` | `resolveDirectiveOnlyTtsCaptionText` parser helper to extract clean caption from directive-only responses |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.ts` | `captionedFinalText: true` capability declaration |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts` | Test for capability declaration |
| `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` | JSDoc for `captionedFinalText` capability contract |
| `src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts` | Capability seam test |
| `docs/tools/tts.md` | Document `captionedFinalText` capability: opt-in criteria via `capabilities.tts.voice`, tagged mode behavior, text-only fallback, plugin API snippet |
| `docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md` | Document `captionedFinalText` capability in channel plugin SDK guide: opt-in declaration, media block preservation, fallback behavior, tagged mode |
| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Updated SDK API baseline hash |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts
 ✓ 202 passed (202)

$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts
 ✓ 29 passed (29)

$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ 50 passed (50)

$ npx vitest run extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts
 ✓ 11 passed (11)

$ npx vitest run src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts
 ✓ 1 passed (1)

Total: 293 tests passed across 5 test suites
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram TTS final-mode text-before-voice churn on both ACP and non-ACP dispatch paths, plus tagged mode partial preview leaking raw `[[tts:text]]` directives, plus media block suppression during captioned final TTS.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at commit `05b4483a8f`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Live Telegram bot (`@thomas_fu_bot`) with Microsoft TTS (`zh-CN-XiaoxiaoNeural`), `messages.tts.mode: "final"`.

- **Failing proof before fix (live Telegram)**:

  ![Before fix — separate text message + voice message (churn)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-before-fix.png)

  User sent "hello" to the Telegram bot. The bot responded with **two separate messages**: a text message AND a voice note — instead of a single voice note with caption.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Built and installed from current head: `npm run build && npm pack && npm install -g openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz`
  2. Registered and restarted Gateway: `openclaw gateway install && openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Tested with `messages.tts.auto: "always"` and `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"` — sent plain messages and directive-bearing messages to `@thomas_fu_bot` on Telegram

- **Evidence after fix**: Always mode (`messages.tts.auto: "always"`) at commit `05b4483a8f`:

  ![Always mode — voice with caption, single message](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-always-mode.png)

  Always-mode replies produce a single voice note with caption text. No text churn, no separate text bubble.

- **Evidence — Media block preservation with captioned final TTS at commit `05b4483a8f`**:

  ![Media block preserved — image delivered + voice note with caption](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-media-block.png)

  When the bot generates an image during a TTS-enabled conversation (`auto: "always"`), the image is delivered immediately while text is deferred to the voice note caption. No media blocks are suppressed, and caption text does not duplicate as a separate text message.

- **Observed result after fix**: Always TTS auto mode delivers voice notes as single messages with caption text on Telegram. Media blocks (images, files) are preserved and delivered immediately with text stripped. No text flash, no duplicate messages, no media loss.

- **What was not tested**: Other TTS providers (only Microsoft TTS tested live). Group chat TTS (tested in DM only). Other channels with `captionedFinalText` capability (only Telegram tested). Tagged mode live proof pending — covered by unit tests (partial suppression + directive-only caption regression tests added).

Closes #83511

target.title: fix(memory-core): index memory path in FTS text for filename queries (fixes #94102)
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #94102.
- `memory_search` FTS rows indexed only chunk body text, so queries for date-like filename stems (e.g. `2026-06-17-1649`) returned unrelated files with `textScore: 0` even when the target file was indexed and content search worked.
- Include the memory file path in the FTS `text` column at index time (`${entry.path}\n${chunk.text}`) so filename/date tokens participate in hybrid keyword ranking.
- Vector embeddings and chunk body text are unchanged.
- Keyword-search snippets and lexical density scoring strip the indexed path prefix back off, so excerpts show the chunk body (not the path line) and stay consistent with vector-search snippets; the FTS index keeps the path purely for matching.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Exact filename/date-token queries fail to rank the matching memory file first despite a clean index and working content search.
- Real environment tested: Linux (WSL2) source checkout, Node 22, FTS-only memory backend (`provider: none`, vector disabled).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built the local checkout (`pnpm build`).
  2. Ran a redacted behavior probe that indexes two memory files and executes `memory_search` for `2026-06-17-1649` through the bundled memory-core runtime.
- Evidence after fix:
  Terminal transcript:
  ```text
  $ node behavior-probe-memory-filename.mjs
  {
    "top": [
      {
        "path": "memory/2026-06-17-1649.md",
        "textScore": 0.000003905989965114915,
        "score": 1
      }
    ]
  }
  ```

  Snippet-cleanliness guard (run via `node`), asserting the keyword hit returns the chunk body and not the `memory/...` path line:

  ```text
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts
  ✓ searchKeyword filename token ranking (issue #94102) > matches date-like filename stems when path is included in FTS indexed text
    expect(results[0].snippet).toBe("Token has been expired or revoked Google OAuth Testing mode")
    expect(results[0].snippet.startsWith("memory/")).toBe(false)
  Tests  23 passed (23)
  ```
- Observed result after fix: Top result path is `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md` with `textScore > 0`; nearby file `memory/2026-06-17-1701.md` is not returned for the filename stem query. The returned snippet for that hit is the chunk body (`Token has been expired or revoked Google OAuth Testing mode`), not the path-prefixed FTS text.
- What was not tested: Live `openclaw memory search` with Gemini embeddings against a production workspace (requires provider credentials and reindex).

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts` — 23 passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:extensions` — exit 0.
- Regression coverage: filename/date stem ranks first with `textScore > 0`, and the keyword snippet equals the chunk body (asserts it does not start with the `memory/` path prefix).

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? Yes
Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No
Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No
Highest-risk area:
Hybrid memory search keyword ranking for existing indexes
Mitigation:
Change applies on reindex/write paths only; no config surface. Existing indexes need reindex to pick up path tokens in FTS text.

## Current review state

Next action:
Await CI + ClawSweeper review
Waiting on:
Maintainer review
Bot comments addressed:
Updated Real behavior proof after initial mock-only-proof gate failure

AI-assisted: yes
